# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define exoelectrogens. List any four characteristics of an ideal microorganism for use in a Microbial Fuel Cell.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (1 mark):** Exoelectrogens are microorganisms capable of transferring electrons extracellularly to insoluble external electron acceptors such as the anode of an MFC.

> [!NOTE]
> **Any four characteristics (2 marks — 0.5 each):**
> 1. **Electroactive** — must possess mechanisms (cytochromes, pili, or mediators) for extracellular electron transfer (EET).
> 2. **Substrate versatility** — ability to oxidize a wide range of organic substrates.
> 3. **Anaerobic or facultatively anaerobic** — functional in the oxygen-depleted anode chamber.
> 4. **Stable biofilm-forming ability** — strong adhesion to the anode surface for sustained DET.
> 5. **High Coulombic efficiency** — minimal electron diversion to side reactions like methanogenesis.
> 6. **Non-pathogenic and fast-growing** — suitable for scalable, safe deployment.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`
**Differentiate between pure cultures and mixed cultures as biocatalysts in MFCs.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> | Basis | Pure Culture | Mixed Culture |
> |---|---|---|
> | **Source** | Laboratory culture collections | Wastewater, sludge, sediment |
> | **Cost** | High (sterile media, aseptic operation) | Low (no sterilization required) |
> | **Coulombic Efficiency** | High (40–95 %) | Low (10–40 %) due to methanogens |
> | **Substrate range** | Narrow (specific to one organism) | Broad (syntrophic degradation) |
> | **Operational stability** | Lower (vulnerable to contamination) | High (resilient consortium) |
> | **Example** | *Geobacter sulfurreducens* | Anaerobic sludge from WWTP |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the two principal mechanisms of **extracellular electron transfer (EET)** in MFC microorganisms. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **Coulombic Efficiency (CE)** expression for an acetate-fed MFC operated with *Geobacter sulfurreducens*. Given: 0.5 g of acetate is consumed, average current = 25 mA over 24 hours. Calculate the CE. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution (a):**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **EET Mechanism — Two Modes:**

**1. Direct Electron Transfer (DET):** The bacterial outer-membrane c-type cytochromes (e.g., **OmcB, OmcE, OmcS, OmcZ** in *Geobacter*) physically contact the anode and transfer electrons directly. Additionally, **conductive type IV pili (nanowires)** — protein appendages with metallic-like conductivity (~0.1 S/cm) — extend from the cell to distant electrode surfaces, enabling long-range DET.

**2. Mediated Electron Transfer (MET):** Microorganisms secrete or utilize **soluble redox mediators** (endogenous — flavins, phenazines, quinones in *Shewanella* and *Pseudomonas*; or exogenous — neutral red, methylene blue). The mediator is **reduced** by the microbe, diffuses to the anode, becomes **re-oxidized** at the electrode, and recycles. This allows even planktonic (non-adhered) cells to contribute electrons.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Difference Table (board exam favorite):**
> - **DET:** No diffusion loss → higher CE; requires biofilm.
> - **MET:** Mediator-dependent; works with suspended cells; lower CE due to mediator wash-out.

#### **Model Solution (b):**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Coulombic Efficiency Derivation:**

**Step 1:** Half-reaction for acetate oxidation:
$$
\text{CH}_3\text{COO}^- + 4\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow 2\text{HCO}_3^- + 9\text{H}^+ + 8\text{e}^-
$$
So $b = 8$ electrons per mole of acetate.

**Step 2:** Substrate mass $q = 0.5 \text{ g}$, Molecular weight $M = 59 \text{ g/mol}$.

**Step 3:** Theoretical charge:

$$
Q_{theory} = \frac{b \cdot q \cdot F}{M} = \frac{8 \times 0.5 \times 96{,}485}{59}
$$

**Step 4:** Simplify:

$$
Q_{theory} = \frac{385{,}940}{59} \approx 6541.36 \text{ C}
$$

**Step 5:** Actual charge:

$$
Q_{actual} = I \cdot t = 0.025 \times (24 \times 3600) = 0.025 \times 86400 = 2160 \text{ C}
$$

**Step 6:** Coulombic Efficiency:

$$
CE = \frac{2160}{6541.36} \times 100\% \approx 33.02\%
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Alert:** Students commonly confuse **current × hours** without converting hours to seconds (factor of 3600). This single mistake yields CE values 3600× too low (~ 0.009 %) and guarantees full marks loss. **Always convert time to seconds before computing charge.**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

**(a)** Discuss the **availability and characteristic features** of *Geobacter sulfurreducens* and *Shewanella oneidensis* as MFC biocatalysts. Compare their electron transfer mechanisms. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Why are **mixed microbial consortia** preferred over pure cultures for practical MFC applications? Explain with reference to substrate versatility, methanogenesis suppression, and operational robustness. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution (a):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Geobacter sulfurreducens:**
> - **Availability:** Isolated from Potomac River sediments; available from DSMZ.
> - **Type:** Gram-negative, δ-Proteobacteria, strict anaerobe.
> - **Substrate:** Acetate (preferred), formate, lactate.
> - **EET Mechanism:** **DET** via c-type cytochromes (OmcZ) and conductive pili.
> - **Power Density:** Up to 2.0 W/m²; CE 80–95 %.

> [!NOTE]
> **Shewanella oneidensis:**
> - **Availability:** Isolated from Lake Oneida, NY; widely cultured.
> - **Type:** Gram-negative, γ-Proteobacteria, facultative anaerobe.
> - **Substrate:** Lactate, pyruvate, formate, glucose.
> - **EET Mechanism:** **MET** via endogenous flavin mediators (riboflavin, FMN).
> - **Power Density:** ~0.5 W/m²; CE 30–60 %.

**Comparison:** *Geobacter* outperforms *Shewanella* in CE due to DET (no diffusion losses), but *Shewanella* offers greater substrate flexibility and oxygen tolerance, making it suitable for cathode biofilms.

#### **Model Solution (b):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Reasons for Mixed Consortia Preference:**
> 1. **Substrate Versatility:** Real wastewater contains complex organics (proteins, lipids, cellulose). Single pure cultures cannot degrade all; consortia enable **syntrophic degradation** — fermenters produce simple acids → exoelectrogens oxidize them.
> 2. **Methanogenesis Suppression:** Methanogens compete for acetate/H₂, reducing CE. Methods: **heat pretreatment** (kills methanogens but preserves spores of exoelectrogens), **2-bromoethanesulfonate (BES)** inhibition, or **short hydraulic retention time**.
> 3. **Operational Robustness:** Mixed consortia tolerate fluctuations in pH, temperature, and substrate concentration — critical for real wastewater treatment plants where conditions vary daily.
> 4. **Cost-Effectiveness:** No sterile media or aseptic equipment needed; inoculation directly from WWTP sludge.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Alert:** When asked "Why mixed cultures?", students often write only "they are cheaper" without elaborating on **syntrophic interactions, biofilm resilience, and pretreatment strategies**. This typically forfeits 3–4 marks. Always include **at least three distinct technical reasons** with examples.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — "Availability and Characteristics of MFC Microorganisms"**
>
> ✅ **Exoelectrogens** are microbes that transfer electrons extracellularly to anodes.
>
> ✅ **Key pure cultures:** *Geobacter sulfurreducens* (DET via cytochromes + nanowires, high CE), *Shewanella oneidensis* (MET via flavins, moderate CE), *Pseudomonas aeruginosa* (MET via phenazines), *Rhodoferax ferrireducens*.
>
> ✅ **Natural availability sources:** Wastewater, anaerobic digester sludge, marine/river sediments, paddy field soil.
>
> ✅ **Two EET mechanisms:** **DET** (no mediator; requires contact) vs. **MET** (uses redox shuttles; works for planktonic cells).
>
> ✅ **Mixed cultures preferred in practice** due to substrate breadth, robustness, low cost — but lower CE due to methanogenic losses.
>
> ✅ **Methanogenesis suppression:** Heat pretreatment, BES inhibitor, short HRT.
>
> ✅ **Must-memorize constants:** $F = 96{,}485 \text{ C/mol}$, $R = 8.314 \text{ J/mol·K}$, $T = 298.15 \text{ K}$, $RT/F \approx 25.7 \text{ mV}$.
>
> ✅ **CE formula:** $CE = \dfrac{M \cdot I \cdot t}{F \cdot b \cdot q} \times 100\%$
>
> ✅ **Power density formula:** $P_d = V \cdot I / A$ (W/m²)
>
> ✅ **Theoretical MFC voltage:** ~1.23 V (glucose); **Practical observed:** 0.3–0.7 V due to overpotentials.
>
> ✅ **OmcZ** = most critical cytochrome in *Geobacter* for high-current EET.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Energy Yields from the Conversion of Energy Crops to Biofuels: Challenges in Applying Sustainable Bioenergy Systems and Their Further Development
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBBT721 - MICROBIAL FUEL CELL
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Energy Yields from the Conversion of Energy Crops to Biofuels: Challenges in Applying Sustainable Bioenergy Systems and Their Further Development

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Energy Crops and Biofuels

**Energy Crops** are dedicated lignocellulosic, starch-based, or sugar-based plant species cultivated primarily as a renewable feedstock for the production of bioenergy carriers such as bioethanol, biodiesel, biogas, and biohydrogen. Unlike food crops, they are selected for high biomass yield per hectare, low input requirements, and ability to grow on marginal lands.

**Biofuels** are gaseous, liquid, or solid fuels derived from recently fixed organic carbon (biomass). They are classified by source (1st, 2nd, 3rd, 4th generation) and by end-use (transportation, heating, electricity).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor:** Bioenergy conversion efficiency is fundamentally limited by the **photosynthetic efficiency ceiling (~3–6% of total incident solar radiation** for C3 crops; up to **~8% for C4 crops like sugarcane and maize**), which dictates the maximum theoretical energy yield per hectare.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Solar Savings Account"

Think of an energy crop as a **savings account powered by sunlight**. The plant is the bank — every photon of sunlight it captures through photosynthesis is a "deposit" of chemical energy stored in the form of carbohydrates (glucose, starch, cellulose, lignin). When we convert this biomass into biofuel, we are essentially **withdrawing money from the account, but only in the currency the engine accepts** (ethanol, methane, hydrogen). The challenge is that every "transaction" (conversion step — fermentation, anaerobic digestion, gasification) incurs a **transaction fee in the form of energy losses** (heat, by-products, incomplete conversion). A sustainable bioenergy system is one where the **net withdrawal rate does not exceed the net deposit rate**, otherwise the "account" depletes (soil carbon loss, deforestation, food-vs-fuel conflict).

## 1.3 Classification of Biofuels — The "Generation Timeline"

| Generation | Feedstock | Conversion Route | Example | Status |
|:-----------|:----------|:-----------------|:--------|:-------|
| **1st Gen** | Sugar/starch/vegetable oil | Fermentation, transesterification | Bioethanol from sugarcane; Biodiesel from soybean | Commercial |
| **2nd Gen** | Lignocellulosic biomass | Pretreatment + enzymatic hydrolysis / Fischer-Tropsch | Cellulosic ethanol from switchgrass | Demonstration |
| **3rd Gen** | Algae (microalgae) | Lipid extraction, hydrothermal liquefaction | Algal biodiesel | Pilot |
| **4th Gen** | Genetically engineered organisms | Electro-fuels, synthetic biology | Solar-to-fuel via engineered cyanobacteria | Research |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters for MFC context:** Microbial Fuel Cells can directly convert a wide spectrum of organic substrates (including 2nd/3rd generation feedstocks and their hydrolysates, wastewaters, and residues) into electricity via microbial catabolism, bypassing combustion and offering a higher theoretical conversion efficiency (typically **~40–60% Coulombic efficiency** under optimized lab conditions vs. **~25–30%** for internal combustion engines).

## 1.4 Energy Yield — The Governing Metric

The **Energy Yield (Y_E)** of a bioenergy pathway is defined as the usable energy output per unit land area per unit time. The KTU-relevant benchmark metric is:

$$Y_E = E_{\text{biofuel}} \times \eta_{\text{conversion}} \times \rho_{\text{biomass}} \times \text{LAI}$$

where $E_{\text{biofuel}}$ is the energy content of the biofuel (MJ/kg), $\eta_{\text{conversion}}$ is the conversion efficiency, $\rho_{\text{biomass}}$ is the biomass yield (kg/ha·yr), and LAI is the Light Availability Index (dimensionless, 0–1).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Logic: From Solar Radiation to Liquid Fuel

**Step 1 — Capture (Photosynthesis):** Chloroplasts in C3/C4 plants fix atmospheric CO₂ via the Calvin–Benson cycle, converting solar photons into chemical bond energy. Maximum theoretical photosynthetic efficiency is bounded by:

$$\eta_{\text{photo, max}} = \frac{\Delta G^{\circ}_{\text{glucose}}}{E_{\text{photon}} \times N_{\text{photons}}}$$

The stoichiometric requirement is **8–10 photons per molecule of CO₂ fixed** (the classical minimum, with PAR = 400–700 nm).

**Step 2 — Accumulation (Biomass Growth):** The crop partitions photosynthate into harvestable tissue (sugars, starch, oil, lignocellulose). Net Primary Productivity (NPP) for terrestrial crops ranges **0.5–2.5 kg dry matter/m²/year** for high-yield energy crops like sugarcane and Miscanthus.

**Step 3 — Pretreatment (for 2nd Gen):** Lignocellulose (cellulose + hemicellulose + lignin) is recalcitrant. Physical (milling), chemical (dilute acid/alkali), physico-chemical (steam explosion), or biological (white-rot fungi) pretreatments are required to liberate fermentable sugars. Typical **yields after pretreatment + enzymatic hydrolysis: 70–90%** of theoretical glucan.

**Step 4 — Conversion (Biochemical / Thermochemical):**
- **Fermentation:** C₆H₁₂O₆ → 2 C₂H₅OH + 2 CO₂ (bioethanol)
- **Anaerobic Digestion:** Organic matter → CH₄ + CO₂ + H₂S (biogas)
- **Transesterification:** Triglyceride + 3 MeOH → 3 FAME + glycerol (biodiesel)
- **Gasification:** CₓHᵧOᵤ + O₂ → CO + H₂ (syngas → Fischer-Tropsch diesel)

**Step 5 — Upgrading & Distribution:** Distillation, dehydration, blending (E10, E85, B20).

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are KTU 2024 high-yield. Master these for ESE and internal assessments.**

| # | Formula | Description | Typical Value / Unit |
|:--|:--------|:------------|:--------------------|
| 1 | $E_{\text{ethanol}} = 29.7 \text{ MJ/kg}$ | Lower heating value (LHV) of bioethanol | MJ/kg |
| 2 | $E_{\text{biodiesel}} = 37.2 \text{ MJ/kg}$ | LHV of FAME biodiesel | MJ/kg |
| 3 | $E_{\text{methane}} = 50.0 \text{ MJ/kg}$ | LHV of CH₄ from biogas | MJ/kg |
| 4 | $E_{\text{gasoline}} = 44.0 \text{ MJ/kg}$ | Reference fossil comparator | MJ/kg |
| 5 | $\eta_{\text{1st-gen}} = 25\text{–}35\%$ | Fermentation → distillation efficiency | dimensionless |
| 6 | $\eta_{\text{2nd-gen}} = 15\text{–}25\%$ | Lignocellulosic ethanol overall efficiency | dimensionless |
| 7 | $\eta_{\text{biodiesel}} = 80\text{–}90\%$ | Transesterification mass conversion | dimensionless |
| 8 | $\eta_{\text{AD}} = 30\text{–}60\%$ | Anaerobic digestion CH₄ yield (VS basis) | dimensionless |
| 9 | $\eta_{\text{MFC}} = 5\text{–}40\%$ | Coulombic efficiency of MFC | dimensionless |
| 10 | $Y_E = \frac{E_{\text{out}}}{A \cdot t}$ | Energy yield (GJ/ha·yr) | GJ·ha⁻¹·yr⁻¹ |
| 11 | $\text{NER} = \frac{E_{\text{out}}}{E_{\text{in}}}$ | Net Energy Ratio (must be $>1$) | dimensionless |
| 12 | $\text{GHG}_{\text{saving}} = 1 - \frac{GHG_{\text{bio}}}{GHG_{\text{fossil}}}$ | Lifecycle GHG savings | dimensionless |
| 13 | $n_{\text{ATP}}^{\text{ethanol}} = 2$ | ATP yield per glucose (fermentation) | mol ATP/mol glu |
| 14 | $\eta_{\text{thermo}} = 1 - \frac{T_0}{T_H}$ | Carnot efficiency ceiling | dimensionless |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

This knowledge is the **foundational feedstock-side analysis** that directly informs Microbial Fuel Cell engineering design. The substrate diversity of MFCs (acetate, glucose, wastewater, lignocellulosic hydrolysate, algal biomass) is dictated by the upstream bioenergy supply chain. Understanding which crops yield which intermediates and at what stoichiometric purity enables the engineer to:

1. **Match substrate to anode biofilm** (Geobacter sulfurreducens prefers acetate; Shewanella prefers lactate).
2. **Predict MFC power output** via the Nernst-derived electromotive force equation ($E_{cell} = E_{cathode} - E_{anode}$).
3. **Design hybrid systems** (e.g., anaerobic digester + MFC + algal photobioreactor for closed-loop nutrient and energy recovery).

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** A sugarcane-based bioethanol plant producing **~85 L ethanol/tonne cane** with **~10 tonnes cane/ha·yr** yields approximately **~25–30 GJ/ha·yr** of liquid fuel energy — comparable to a 1 kW solar PV installation operating at **15% capacity factor** over the same area. This benchmark is essential for land-use policy debates.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Theoretical Photosynthetic Energy Yield Ceiling

**Goal:** Derive the maximum theoretical energy conversion efficiency of a C3 plant under standard AM 1.5 solar irradiance.

**Given:**
- Standard solar flux at Earth's surface: $I_0 = 1000 \text{ W/m}^2$ (AM 1.5G)
- Average photon wavelength in PAR band: $\lambda = 550 \text{ nm}$ (green peak)
- Gibbs free energy of glucose synthesis: $\Delta G^{\circ} = +2870 \text{ kJ/mol}$
- Number of photons required per CO₂ fixed (theoretical minimum): $n = 8$ photons
- Avogadro's number: $N_A = 6.022 \times 10^{23} \text{ mol}^{-1}$
- Planck's constant: $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34} \text{ J·s}$
- Speed of light: $c = 3.0 \times 10^{8} \text{ m/s}$

**Step 1:** Compute the energy of a single photon at $\lambda = 550 \text{ nm}$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{\text{photon}} &= \frac{h \cdot c}{\lambda} \\
&= \frac{6.626 \times 10^{-34} \times 3.0 \times 10^{8}}{550 \times 10^{-9}} \\
&= \frac{1.9878 \times 10^{-25}}{5.50 \times 10^{-7}} \\
&= 3.614 \times 10^{-19} \text{ J/photon}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Convert photon energy to kJ/mol using Avogadro's number.

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{\text{photon, mol}} &= 3.614 \times 10^{-19} \times 6.022 \times 10^{23} \\
&= 2.176 \times 10^{5} \text{ J/mol} \\
&= 217.6 \text{ kJ/mol}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Compute the minimum solar energy required to fix 1 mol of CO₂ (8 photons).

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{\text{input}} &= n \times E_{\text{photon, mol}} = 8 \times 217.6 \\
&= 1740.8 \text{ kJ/mol CO}_2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Compute the theoretical maximum photosynthetic efficiency.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_{\text{photo, max}} &= \frac{\Delta G^{\circ}}{E_{\text{input}}} = \frac{2870}{1740.8} \\
&= 1.648 \quad \text{(dimensionless, } > 1 \text{!?)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall Alert:** The ratio exceeding 1 indicates the analysis assumed all photons are at the optimal wavelength. Accounting for the full PAR spectral distribution and the Z-scheme charge separation (which uses 4 photons per CO₂ but in two pairs at distinct wavelengths), the **realistic ceiling is ~11%** for the photon-to-chemical step, and only **~3–6%** is realized by real C3 crops when all solar losses (reflection, non-PAR wavelengths, respiration, photorespiration) are included.

**Step 5 (Corrected) — Realistic ceiling with spectral fraction:**

Let the PAR fraction of incident solar energy be **45%**, and the average quantum yield be **0.06 mol C/mol photons** (accounting for photorespiration).

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_{\text{realistic}} &= 0.45 \times 0.06 \times \frac{2870 \text{ kJ/mol}}{1740.8 \text{ kJ/mol}} \\
&\approx 0.044 \quad \text{or} \quad 4.4\%
\end{aligned}
$$

This matches the empirically observed **3–6% range** for productive C3 crops. ✓

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Net Energy Ratio (NER) for Sugarcane Bioethanol

**Goal:** Demonstrate NER $> 1$ for sugarcane ethanol, justifying its commercial viability.

**Assumed Inputs (per hectare-year for Brazilian-style production):**
- Synthetic N fertilizer: **150 kg N** → **E_N = 50 MJ/kg N applied** (life-cycle)
- Diesel for agricultural machinery: **80 L/ha**
- Process energy (fermentation, distillation): **8 GJ/ha**
- Biomass production: **80 tonnes cane/ha·yr** at 27% recoverable sugar = **21.6 tonnes sugar**

**Step 1:** Compute agricultural energy input.

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{\text{agri}} &= (150 \times 50) + (80 \times 38.6 \text{ MJ/L}) \\
&= 7500 + 3088 = 10588 \text{ MJ/ha·yr} \approx 10.6 \text{ GJ/ha·yr}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Compute total input including processing.

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{\text{in, total}} &= 10.6 + 8 = 18.6 \text{ GJ/ha·yr}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Compute ethanol output (theoretical yield = 0.51 g ethanol / g glucose; practical = 0.46).

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{\text{ethanol}} &= 21600 \times 0.46 = 9936 \text{ kg/ha·yr} \approx 9.94 \text{ tonnes} \\
E_{\text{out}} &= 9936 \times 29.7 \text{ MJ/kg} = 295100 \text{ MJ/ha·yr} \approx 295 \text{ GJ/ha·yr}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Compute NER.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{NER} &= \frac{295}{18.6} \approx 15.9
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** Sugarcane bioethanol yields **~16 units of energy out per unit in**, a strongly favorable thermodynamic case.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Biofuel Pathways

| Pathway | Feedstock | $E_{\text{in}}$ (GJ/ha·yr) | $E_{\text{out}}$ (GJ/ha·yr) | NER | $\eta_{\text{lifecycle}}$ | $\text{GHG}_{\text{saving}}$ |
|:--------|:----------|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| Sugarcane ethanol | Sugarcane | 18.6 | 295 | **15.9** | ~30% | ~70% |
| Corn ethanol | Maize | 28.5 | 142 | **5.0** | ~22% | ~40% |
| Cellulosic ethanol | Switchgrass | 22.0 | 180 | **8.2** | ~28% | ~85% |
| Soy biodiesel | Soybean | 18.0 | 55 | **3.1** | ~18% | ~50% |
| Palm biodiesel | Palm oil | 14.0 | 220 | **15.7** | ~30% | ~60%* |
| Biogas (AD) | Maize silage | 25.0 | 165 | **6.6** | ~35% | ~75% |
| Microbial Fuel Cell (wastewater) | Acetate | 0.5 (electrical aux.) | 12 (electricity) | **~24** | ~10% | ~90% |

\*Palm biodiesel GHG saving is highly contested due to land-use change (peat drainage, deforestation).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->
