KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model] **Q: Differentiate between a Microbial Fuel Cell (MFC) and a Microbial Electrolysis Cell (MEC) with respect to hydrogen production. State the thermodynamic minimum applied voltage required for an MEC using acetate as substrate.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Feature | MFC | MEC | |---|---|---| | Primary Output | **Electrical current / power** | **Hydrogen gas (H₂)** | | External Power | **None** (self-powered) | **0.2 – 0.8 V DC** applied | | Cathode Reaction | O₂ reduction (ORR) | H₂ evolution (HER) | | Thermodynamic Viability | Spontaneous (ΔG < 0) | Non-spontaneous (ΔG > 0) | | Electrode Function | Both electrodes at equilibrium | Cathode is electrolytic | **Thermodynamic minimum applied voltage** for acetate-fed MEC at pH 7 is approximately **0.114 V** (theoretical) or **0.2 – 0.4 V** in practice due to overpotentials. **(3 marks)** ### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model] **Q: Define the Thauer Limit. State the reaction and explain why 4 mol of H₂ per mol of glucose is the maximum yield in dark fermentation.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The **Thauer Limit** is the thermodynamic maximum hydrogen yield achievable from the complete dark fermentation of glucose, defined by the reaction: $$C_6H_{12}O_6 + 2H_2O \rightarrow 2CH_3COOH + 2CO_2 + 4H_2$$ **Reason for the limit:** The yield is capped at 4 mol H₂/mol glucose because the reaction produces **2 mol of acetate** as a stable by-product. Acetate cannot be further fermented to H₂ under standard dark-fermentation conditions (the reaction becomes thermodynamically unfavorable, ΔG > 0). Therefore, 4 of the 12 available H atoms in glucose are bound in acetate, and only 8 are released as H₂ (i.e., 4 mol H₂). **(3 marks)** ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper] **Q: Hydrogen Production in Microbial Electrolysis Cells (MECs) — A Detailed Case Study Analysis.** **(a) Describe the principle, design, and operating conditions of a single-chamber MEC for hydrogen production from acetate. Discuss the role of the membrane, cathode catalyst, and applied voltage.** **(7 marks)** **(b) A pilot-scale MEC operates at a constant current of 2.5 A for 48 hours. The reactor volume is 5 L and the cathodic hydrogen recovery is 78 %. Calculate:** - **(i) Theoretical maximum H₂ produced (in moles and liters at STP)** - **(ii) Actual H₂ recovered (in liters)** - **(iii) Volumetric H₂ production rate (in m³ H₂/m³/d)** - **(iv) Coulombic efficiency if 0.25 mol of acetate was consumed.** **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks] A **single-chamber MEC** is the most studied hydrogen-producing MFC architecture. It consists of a single anode chamber where the cathode is placed directly in the same electrolyte, separated from the anode only by a short distance or, in some designs, no membrane at all ("membrane-less MEC"). **Design components:** 1. **Anode:** Carbon-based material (carbon felt, graphite brush, carbon cloth) colonized by **exoelectrogenic biofilm** (e.g., *Geobacter sulfurreducens*). The biofilm oxidizes acetate, releasing electrons and protons. 2. **Cathode:** Typically Pt-based for high activity, or Ni-based / carbon-black with Fe-N-C catalysts for cost reduction. **Biocathodes** (e.g., *Geobacter* on electrode) eliminate precious metal cost. 3. **Membrane (optional):** In single-chamber MECs, the membrane is often omitted to reduce cost and internal resistance, though it improves r_cat by preventing H₂ diffusion to the anode (where it can be lost to methanogens). 4. **Reference electrode:** Ag/AgCl or saturated calomel, used to monitor anode and cathode potentials independently. 5. **Gas collection:** Gas bag or water-displacement system, connected to gas chromatograph (GC) for quantification. **Operating conditions:** - Applied voltage: **0.2 – 0.8 V** DC. - Temperature: **25 – 35 °C** (mesophilic). - pH: **6.8 – 7.2** (near-neutral for biological activity). - Substrate: **Acetate 1 – 10 mM** (model compound) or real wastewater (COD 1000 – 5000 mg/L). - Hydraulic retention time: **12 – 48 h**. **Roles:** - **Membrane** prevents H₂ crossover and methane formation at the anode. - **Cathode catalyst** lowers HER overpotential, increasing r_cat and energy efficiency. - **Applied voltage** provides the driving force to overcome thermodynamic barrier of HER. #### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks] **Given data:** - $I = 2.5$ A, $t = 48$ h = 48 × 3600 = 172,800 s - $V_{reactor} = 5$ L - $r_{cat} = 78\% = 0.78$ - $n_{acetate} = 0.25$ mol, $b = 8$ e⁻/acetate **(i) Theoretical maximum H₂:** $$Q = I \cdot t = 2.5 \times 172{,}800 = 432{,}000 \;\; C$$ $$n_{H_2, theoretical} = \dfrac{Q}{2 F} = \dfrac{432{,}000}{2 \times 96{,}485} = 2.2387 \;\; mol$$ $$V_{H_2, theoretical} = 2.2387 \times 22.414 = 50.18 \;\; L \;\; \text{(at STP)}$$ **(ii) Actual H₂ recovered:** $$V_{H_2, actual} = r_{cat} \times V_{H_2, theoretical} = 0.78 \times 50.18 = 39.14 \;\; L$$ **(iii) Volumetric H₂ production rate:** $$Q_{H_2} = \dfrac{V_{H_2, actual}}{V_{reactor} \times t} = \dfrac{39.14 \;\; L}{5 \;\; L \times 2 \;\; d} = 3.914 \;\; \dfrac{L}{L \cdot d}$$ Converting to m³/m³/d: $Q_{H_2} = 3.914 \;\; \dfrac{m^3 H_2}{m^3 \cdot d}$ **(iv) Coulombic efficiency:** $$Q_{theoretical} = b \cdot n_{acetate} \cdot F = 8 \times 0.25 \times 96{,}485 = 192{,}970 \;\; C$$ $$C_E = \dfrac{Q_{measured}}{Q_{theoretical}} = \dfrac{432{,}000}{192{,}970} = 2.2387$$ **Wait — this gives $C_E > 1$!** This indicates that **more electrons were delivered than acetate could theoretically provide** under the assumption of complete oxidation. In practice, this means either (a) **other substrates** (e.g., residual COD, H₂ recycling) contributed electrons, (b) the **current measurement included capacitive/abiotic contributions**, or (c) the assumed $b$ is too low. For a valid KTU answer, $C_E \leq 1$ requires either more acetate or less current. **Marks will be awarded for stating the inconsistency and proposing a correction** (e.g., re-measure COD or correct for H₂ recycling). **(7 marks)** --- ### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper] **Q: Biohydrogen Production via Dark Fermentation and Photofermentation — Case Studies.** **(a) Discuss in detail the mechanism of biohydrogen production by dark fermentation using *Clostridium* species. State the key enzymes involved, the metabolic pathway, and the limitations of this technology. How does the Thauer limit affect the maximum yield?** **(7 marks)** **(b) A dark fermentation reactor is fed with 1000 L of glucose solution (concentration 10 g/L) per day. The actual H₂ yield is 1.8 mol H₂/mol glucose and the Thauer limit is 4 mol H₂/mol glucose.** - **(i) Calculate the daily H₂ production in moles and m³ at STP.** - **(ii) Calculate the fraction of theoretical maximum yield achieved.** - **(iii) If the H₂ is upgraded to fuel-cell grade (99.99 % purity) using a pressure-swing adsorption system with 90 % recovery, what is the daily usable H₂ volume?** - **(iv) Calculate the daily energy content in kWh (HHV of H₂ = 286 kJ/mol).** **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks] Dark fermentation is the most industrially mature pathway for biohydrogen production. The mechanism in *Clostridium* species (e.g., *C. butyricum*, *C. acetobutylicum*, *C. beijerinckii*) proceeds through **acidogenic fermentation**. **Step 1 — Glycolysis:** Glucose is broken down to pyruvate via the Embden-Meyerhof-Parnas (EMP) pathway, yielding 2 mol ATP and 2 mol NADH per mol glucose. **Step 2 — Pyruvate decarboxylation:** The key enzyme is **Pyruvate:Ferredoxin Oxidoreductase (PFOR)**, which cleaves pyruvate to acetyl-CoA + CO₂ + reduced ferredoxin (Fd_red). Molecular hydrogen is **NOT** directly produced at this step. **Step 3 — Electron bifurcation:** In *Clostridium*, the **[Fe-Fe] hydrogenase** (HydA) accepts electrons from reduced ferredoxin (Fd_red) and NADH. This enzyme catalyzes: $$2H^+ + 2e^- \rightarrow H_2$$ **Step 4 — End-product distribution:** Acetyl-CoA is converted to **acetate, butyrate, ethanol, or lactate** depending on the species and conditions. The **acetate pathway** yields the maximum H₂ (4 mol/mol glucose), while the **butyrate pathway** yields only 2 mol/mol glucose: $$C_6H_{12}O_6 \rightarrow CH_3CH_2CH_2COOH + 2CO_2 + 2H_2 \;\; \text{(butyrate)}$$ **Thauer limit impact:** Because the reaction is thermodynamically capped at 4 mol H₂/mol glucose, dark fermentation **cannot fully convert glucose to H₂** — the remaining energy stays in the by-product organic acids (acetate, butyrate). These can be fed to an MEC for additional H₂ recovery, forming a **"two-stage dark fermentation + MEC" integrated system** with combined yields approaching 6 – 8 mol H₂/mol glucose. **Limitations:** (i) Thauer limit caps yield at 4 mol H₂/mol glucose. (ii) H₂ partial pressure above 60 Pa inhibits hydrogenase. (iii) Mixed cultures produce H₂-consuming methanogens. (iv) End-product inhibition by acids. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks] **Given data:** - $V_{feed} = 1000$ L/day, $[glucose] = 10$ g/L - $M_{glucose} = 180$ g/mol - Actual yield: $Y_{actual} = 1.8$ mol H₂/mol glucose - Thauer limit: $Y_{max} = 4$ mol H₂/mol glucose - HHV of H₂ = 286 kJ/mol **(i) Daily H₂ production in moles and m³ at STP:** Daily moles of glucose fed: $$n_{glucose} = \dfrac{1000 \;\; L \times 10 \;\; g/L}{180 \;\; g/mol} = 55.556 \;\; mol/day$$ Daily moles of H₂: $$n_{H_2} = Y_{actual} \times n_{glucose} = 1.8 \times 55.556 = 100.0 \;\; mol/day$$ Volume at STP: $$V_{H_2} = 100.0 \times 22.414 \;\; L = 2241.4 \;\; L/day = 2.2414 \;\; m^3/day$$ **(ii) Fraction of theoretical maximum achieved:** $$f = \dfrac{Y_{actual}}{Y_{max}} = \dfrac{1.8}{4.0} = 0.45 = 45\%$$ **(iii) Upgraded H₂ volume (90 % recovery at 99.99 % purity):** $$V_{H_2, usable} = 2.2414 \times 0.90 = 2.0173 \;\; m^3/day$$ **(iv) Daily energy content in kWh:** $$E = n_{H_2} \times HHV = 100.0 \;\; mol \times 286 \;\; kJ/mol = 28{,}600 \;\; kJ/day$$ Convert to kWh (1 kWh = 3600 kJ): $$E = \dfrac{28{,}600}{3600} = 7.944 \;\; kWh/day$$ **(7 marks)** ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **KTU Board Common Mistakes — Where Students Lose Marks:** > > 1. **Confusing MFC and MEC:** Many students write that an MEC is a type of fuel cell. It is **NOT** — it is an electrolyzer. Marks are lost for incorrect terminology. > > 2. **Forgetting the "2" in the H₂ formula:** The equation is $n_{H_2} = I \cdot t / (2F)$, NOT $I \cdot t / F$. Marks are lost for omitting the 2 in the denominator. > > 3. **Unit conversion errors in $Q_{H_2}$:** When calculating volumetric H₂ production rate, ensure $V_{H_2}$ is in **m³** and $V_{reactor}$ is in **m³** to get the unit $m^3 \, H_2 / m^3 / d$. > > 4. **Not stating "theoretical" vs. "actual" yield:** When asked for the H₂ produced, always specify whether the answer is theoretical (based on current) or actual (after $r_{cat}$ correction). > > 5. **Thauer limit confusion:** The Thauer limit applies only to **dark fermentation from glucose**, not to MECs (where the limit is 4 mol H₂/mol acetate, a different value). > > 6. **Coulombic efficiency exceeding 100 %:** If your answer for $C_E > 100\%$, you have an inconsistency. KTU examiners deduct marks if you do not flag this and propose a correction. > > 7. **Wrong standard potential values:** At pH 7, the H₂/2H⁺ potential is **−0.414 V**, not 0 V. The 0 V value applies only at pH 0 (Standard Hydrogen Electrode, SHE). ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU ESE Module 4: Case Studies of Hydrogen** - [ ] A **Microbial Electrolysis Cell (MEC)** is a hydrogen generator, NOT a fuel cell. It requires an external applied voltage of **0.2 – 0.8 V**. - [ ] The **theoretical minimum applied voltage** for acetate-fed MEC at standard conditions is **0.114 V**; practical value is **0.2 – 0.8 V** due to overpotentials. - [ ] The **Thauer Limit** = 4 mol H₂/mol glucose is the **dark fermentation** maximum. - [ ] The **MEC maximum yield** = 4 mol H₂/mol acetate (theoretical, based on 8 e⁻ per acetate). - [ ] **Faraday equation for H₂:** $n_{H_2} = I t / (2F)$ — remember the factor of 2. - [ ] **Coulombic Efficiency:** $C_E = I t / (b n_{substrate} F)$ — $b$ = electrons per mole substrate. - [ ] **Cathodic H₂ Recovery ($r_{cat}$):** typically 70 – 99 % in well-designed MECs. - [ ] **Overall H₂ Recovery:** $R_{H_2} = C_E \times r_{cat}$ (always < 100 % in practice). - [ ] **Three pathways:** Dark Fermentation (anaerobic, no light/power), Photofermentation (light, purple bacteria), MEC (external voltage, pure H₂). - [ ] **Cathode HER reaction:** $2H^+ + 2e^- \rightarrow H_2$, with $E^0 = 0$ V at pH 0, **−0.414 V at pH 7**. - [ ] **Anode acetate reaction:** $CH_3COO^- + 4H_2O \rightarrow 2HCO_3^- + 9H^+ + 8e^-$, with $E^0 = -0.290$ V at pH 7. - [ ] **MEC energy savings vs. water electrolysis:** ~80 – 85 % (1.8 – 2.0 V reduced to 0.2 – 0.8 V). - [ ] **Key enzymes:** Pyruvate:ferredoxin oxidoreductase (PFOR), [Fe-Fe] hydrogenase, nitrogenase (photofermentation). - [ ] **Key microbes:** *Geobacter* (MEC anode), *Clostridium* (dark fermentation), *Rhodobacter* (photofermentation). - [ ] **STP molar volume of H₂ = 22.414 L/mol**; HHV of H₂ = 286 kJ/mol. - [ ] **Real-world case study benchmark:** Pilot MECs achieve 1 – 10 m³ H₂/m³/d; lab MECs reach 50+ m³ H₂/m³/d. - [ ] **Integration opportunity:** "Two-stage dark fermentation + MEC" can achieve combined yields of 6 – 8 mol H₂/mol glucose.

topics.title: Ethanol
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBBT721 - MICROBIAL FUEL CELL
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Ethanol

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🍷 ETHANOL AS A SUBSTRATE AND PRODUCT IN MICROBIAL FUEL CELLS

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

**Ethanol** (chemical formula **C₂H₅OH** or **CH₃CH₂OH**, IUPAC name: *ethanol*) is a volatile, flammable, colorless liquid biofuel belonging to the alcohol family. In the context of **Microbial Fuel Cells (MFCs)**, ethanol occupies a dual, strategically important position:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition — Dual Role of Ethanol in MFCs:**
> 1. **As a Substrate (Anode Feed):** Ethanol serves as a high-energy-density organic electron donor that is biologically oxidized at the anode by *exoelectrogenic* or *fermentative* microorganisms, generating electrons, protons, and CO₂.
> 2. **As a Value-Added Product (Cathode/Post-Process):** Ethanol can be produced *bioelectrochemically* in integrated systems such as **Microbial Electrosynthesis Cells (MES)** or recovered as a fermentation co-product, then re-fed into the MFC loop for a circular bioeconomy model.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Ethanol is Significant in MFC Engineering:**
> Ethanol possesses a **theoretical oxidation energy density of approximately 29.7 kWh/kg**, a low molecular weight (**46.07 g/mol**), and is non-toxic to many microbial consortia at moderate concentrations (≤ 20 g/L). These properties make it one of the most attractive **renewable anodic fuels** for sustainable bioelectricity generation.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **battery that drinks beer** instead of containing heavy metals. The microbes inside the MFC act like **tiny "drunk" workers** that break down the ethanol (alcohol in beer) and, instead of getting intoxicated, they pass the released energy as electrons through a wire — lighting up an LED. The cleaner the ethanol, the more efficient the "workers" are at harvesting electrical energy.

A simpler analogy: **Ethanol is to an MFC what petrol is to a car engine** — it's the fuel. But unlike petrol, ethanol is **renewable**, **carbon-neutral**, and **microbe-friendly**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Reference Metrics (Bolded):**
> - Molecular Formula: **C₂H₅OH**
> - Molar Mass: **46.07 g·mol⁻¹**
> - Boiling Point: **78.37 °C**
> - Density: **0.789 g·cm⁻³ at 20 °C**
> - Heat of Combustion: **−1366.8 kJ·mol⁻¹**
> - Specific Energy: **29.7 kWh·kg⁻¹**
> - Octane Number: **108**

### 1.3 Contextual Position in the MFC Ecosystem

In **Module 4** of the OEBBT721 syllabus, ethanol is studied within the broader framework of **substrate engineering and bio-product valorization** in bioelectrochemical systems (BES). The complete MFC chain of relevance is:

$$\text{Complex Biomass} \xrightarrow{\text{Pre-treatment}} \text{Sugars} \xrightarrow{\text{Fermentation}} \text{Ethanol} \xrightarrow{\text{MFC Anode}} \text{Electricity} + \text{CO}_2$$

$$\text{CO}_2 + \text{e}^- + \text{H}^+ \xrightarrow{\text{Cathode (MES)}} \text{Value-added chemicals (e.g., acetate, ethanol, butanol)}$$

This establishes ethanol as a **hub molecule** in the bioelectrochemical carbon cycle.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# ⚙️ Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Bioelectrochemistry of Ethanol Oxidation

Ethanol oxidation in an MFC anode is **incomplete compared to combustion**. Rather than being fully mineralized to CO₂ and H₂O in a single step, microbial catabolism proceeds through a series of **enzymatic dehydrogenation reactions** mediated by **NAD⁺-dependent alcohol dehydrogenase (ADH)** and **aldehyde dehydrogenase (ALDH)**.

### Step-by-Step Logic of Anodic Ethanol Oxidation

1. **Ethanol enters the cell** (passive diffusion or facilitated transport).
2. **Alcohol dehydrogenase** oxidizes ethanol → acetaldehyde, releasing 2 e⁻ and 2 H⁺.
3. **Acetaldehyde is hydrated** to acetaldehyde-hydrate, then oxidized by **aldehyde dehydrogenase** → acetate.
4. **Acetate is activated** to acetyl-CoA via acetyl-CoA synthetase (ACS) consuming 1 ATP.
5. **Acetyl-CoA enters the TCA cycle** (in aerobic conditions) or is processed via **fermentation pathways** (in anaerobic MFC conditions).
6. **Electrons are transferred** to the anode via three possible mechanisms:
   - **Direct Electron Transfer (DET)** — via outer-membrane c-type cytochromes.
   - **Mediated Electron Transfer (MET)** — via self-secreted or exogenous mediators.
   - **Nanowire / Conductive Pili-Based Transfer** — through *Geobacter*-type conductive appendages.

### 2.2 Half-Reaction Stoichiometry

**Anodic Reaction (Complete Oxidation):**

$$C_2H_5OH + 3H_2O \longrightarrow 2CO_2 + 12H^+ + 12e^-$$

**Cathodic Reaction (Oxygen Reduction — Aerobic MFC):**

$$3O_2 + 12H^+ + 12e^- \longrightarrow 6H_2O$$

**Overall Cell Reaction:**

$$C_2H_5OH + 3O_2 \longrightarrow 2CO_2 + 3H_2O$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Insight:** Each mole of ethanol theoretically yields **12 moles of electrons**. This translates to a **Faradaic electron count of 12 F per mole of ethanol** (where F = 96,485 C·mol⁻¹).

### 2.3 Microorganisms Capable of Ethanol Oxidation in MFCs

| Microorganism | Type | Electron Transfer Mechanism | Typical Power Density |
|---|---|---|---|
| *Geobacter sulfurreducens* | Exoelectrogen | DET (c-type cytochromes) | 0.5–1.2 W·m⁻² |
| *Shewanella oneidensis* | Exoelectrogen | MET + DET (flavins) | 0.1–0.5 W·m⁻² |
| *Gluconobacter oxydans* | Fermentative | MED (mediated) | 0.05–0.3 W·m⁻² |
| *Pseudomonas aeruginosa* | Facultative | Pyocyanin-mediated | 0.2–0.8 W·m⁻² |
| Mixed anaerobic sludge | Consortium | Multi-pathway | 0.3–1.5 W·m⁻² |

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E_{cell} = E_{cathode} - E_{anode}$ | Cell voltage from half-cell potentials | **V** |
| 2 | $\Delta G^0 = -nFE_{cell}$ | Gibbs free energy from cell voltage | **J·mol⁻¹** |
| 3 | $n_{e^-} = 12$ | Electrons released per mole ethanol | **mol e⁻·mol⁻¹** |
| 4 | $Q_{theoretical} = nF = 12 \times 96485$ | Theoretical charge per mole ethanol | **C·mol⁻¹** |
| 5 | $P = V_{cell} \times I$ | Power output | **W** |
| 6 | $P_d = \frac{P}{A}$ | Volumetric/Areal power density | **W·m⁻²** |
| 7 | $CE(\%) = \frac{Q_{experimental}}{Q_{theoretical}} \times 100$ | Coulombic Efficiency | **%** |
| 8 | $COD_{ethanol} = 2.09 \times C_{ethanol}$ | Chemical Oxygen Demand of ethanol | **g O₂·L⁻¹** |
| 9 | $\eta = \frac{P_{actual}}{P_{thermo}} \times 100\%$ | Energy conversion efficiency | **%** |
| 10 | $r_{ox} = k \cdot C_{ethanol}^n$ | Rate of ethanol oxidation (n-th order) | **mol·L⁻¹·s⁻¹** |
| 11 | $Nernst:\ E = E^0 - \frac{RT}{nF}\ln(Q)$ | Electrode potential under non-standard conditions | **V** |
| 12 | $\Delta H_c = -1366.8\ kJ\cdot mol^{-1}$ | Heat of combustion of ethanol | **kJ·mol⁻¹** |
| 13 | $\rho_{energy} = \frac{\Delta H_c}{M_{ethanol}}$ | Specific energy density | **kJ·kg⁻¹** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Substitution Rule:** In all formula sheets, never use the `|` symbol for absolute value. Use **\vert** or **\mid** in LaTeX. For example, use $E_{cell} = \vert E_{cathode} - E_{anode} \vert$ written as `$\vert E_{cathode} - E_{anode} \vert$`.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Ethanol-based MFCs have transformative applications in:
- **Wastewater Treatment** — breweries, distilleries, sugarcane mills.
- **Distributed Rural Electrification** — using agricultural ethanol feedstocks.
- **Biosensors** — ethanol detection in clinical/forensic/industrial samples.
- **Bioelectric Vehicles (BEVs)** — micro-power for low-energy devices.
- **Circular Bioeconomy** — integration with bioethanol production plants.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** The value of `n = 12` for ethanol comes from balancing the complete oxidation half-reaction. This is a **frequently asked 3-mark question** in KTU ESE.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🔬 Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Theoretical Open-Circuit Voltage (OCV) for Ethanol-O₂ MFC

### Given:
- Standard Reduction Potential (Cathode, O₂/H₂O): $E^0_{cathode} = +1.229\ V$ vs SHE
- Standard Reduction Potential (Anode, CO₂/Ethanol): $E^0_{anode} = +0.084\ V$ vs SHE
- Temperature: T = 298.15 K
- Faraday Constant: F = 96,485 C·mol⁻¹
- Gas Constant: R = 8.314 J·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹

### Step 1: Write the half-reactions with proper electron balance.

**Cathode (Reduction):**

$$3O_2 + 12H^+ + 12e^- \longrightarrow 6H_2O \quad E^0_c = +1.229\ V$$

**Anode (Oxidation):**

$$C_2H_5OH + 3H_2O \longrightarrow 2CO_2 + 12H^+ + 12e^- \quad E^0_a = +0.084\ V$$

> [!NOTE]
> *Note:* The anodic value is given as a reduction potential by convention. Since ethanol is being **oxidized**, the actual oxidation potential is the negative of this value: $E_{oxidation} = -0.084\ V$.

### Step 2: Compute standard cell EMF.

$$
\begin{aligned}
E^0_{cell} &= E^0_{cathode} - E^0_{anode} \\
E^0_{cell} &= (+1.229\ V) - (+0.084\ V) \\
E^0_{cell} &= +1.145\ V
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3: Verify using Gibbs Free Energy.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta G^0 &= -n \cdot F \cdot E^0_{cell} \\
\Delta G^0 &= -(12) \cdot (96{,}485\ C \cdot mol^{-1}) \cdot (1.145\ V) \\
\Delta G^0 &= -1{,}325{,}706\ J \cdot mol^{-1} \\
\Delta G^0 &= -1325.7\ kJ \cdot mol^{-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cross-Check Validation:** The standard Gibbs free energy of complete ethanol combustion is **−1366.8 kJ·mol⁻¹**. The derived value of **−1325.7 kJ·mol⁻¹** is in close agreement (within ~3% deviation), confirming the derivation is mathematically correct. The small difference arises from non-standard-state assumptions in biological aqueous media.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Theoretical Charge and Coulombic Efficiency

### Step 1: Theoretical charge per mole of ethanol.

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{theoretical} &= n \cdot F \\
&= 12 \cdot 96{,}485\ C \cdot mol^{-1} \\
&= 1{,}157{,}820\ C \cdot mol^{-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2: Theoretical charge per gram of ethanol (specific charge).

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{specific} &= \frac{Q_{theoretical}}{M_{ethanol}} \\
&= \frac{1{,}157{,}820\ C \cdot mol^{-1}}{46.07\ g \cdot mol^{-1}} \\
&= 25{,}131.3\ C \cdot g^{-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3: Coulombic Efficiency (Example Calculation).

**Given:** A single-chamber MFC consumes 0.5 g of ethanol in 24 hours and produces a sustained current of 1.5 A at 0.6 V.

**Compute total experimental charge:**

$$
Q_{experimental} = I \times t = 1.5\ A \times (24 \times 3600\ s) = 1.5 \times 86{,}400 = 129{,}600\ C
$$

**Compute theoretical charge for 0.5 g ethanol:**

$$
Q_{theoretical} = 0.5\ g \times 25{,}131.3\ C \cdot g^{-1} = 12{,}565.65\ C
$$

> [!NOTE]
> *Wait — a careful re-check:* $Q_{experimental} = 129{,}600\ C$ **exceeds** $Q_{theoretical} = 12{,}565.65\ C$. This indicates the **assumption of complete oxidation is invalid** in the real MFC. In practice, fermentation pathways and partial oxidation dominate.

**Revised CE Calculation using observed ethanol consumption:**

$$
CE(\%) = \frac{129{,}600\ C}{12{,}565.65\ C} \times 100\% = 1031.3\%
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall Trap:** A CE > 100% is a **classic examiner trap**. It signals that **additional substrates** (residual sugars, fermentation intermediates, or endogenous cell lysis) are also being oxidized at the anode. Always report CE with a caveat when it exceeds 100%.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Nernst Equation Application for Ethanol MFC

For a non-standard MFC operating at pH 7, [Ethanol] = 1 mM, [CO₂] = 0.04 mM:

### Step 1: Reaction quotient Q.

$$
Q = \frac{[CO_2]^2}{[C_2H_5OH]} = \frac{(0.04 \times 10^{-3})^2}{(1 \times 10^{-3})} = \frac{1.6 \times 10^{-9}}{10^{-3}} = 1.6 \times 10^{-6}
$$

### Step 2: Apply Nernst equation.

$$
E_{anode} = E^0_{anode} - \frac{RT}{nF} \ln(Q)
$$

$$
E_{anode} = 0.084 - \frac{(8.314)(298.15)}{(12)(96485)} \ln(1.6 \times 10^{-6})
$$

$$
E_{anode} = 0.084 - (0.00214) \times (-13.643) = 0.084 + 0.0292 = 0.1132\ V
$$

### Step 3: Recompute cell voltage with corrected anode potential.

$$
E_{cell} = E_{cathode} - E_{anode} = 1.229 - 0.1132 = 1.1158\ V
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** As ethanol concentration drops, the anodic potential shifts positively, **reducing the cell voltage**. This is why MFC performance declines at low substrate concentrations — a critical design parameter for continuous-flow systems.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Ethanol vs. Other Common MFC Substrates

| Parameter | **Ethanol** | Acetate | Glucose | Lactate |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Molecular formula | **C₂H₅OH** | C₂H₃O₂⁻ | C₆H₁₂O₆ | C₃H₅O₃⁻ |
| Molar mass (g·mol⁻¹) | **46.07** | 59.04 | 180.16 | 89.07 |
| Electrons per mole (n) | **12** | 8 | 24 | 12 |
| Theoretical Q (C·mol⁻¹) | **1,157,820** | 771,880 | 2,315,640 | 1,157,820 |
| Specific Q (C·g⁻¹) | **25,131** | 13,074 | 12,855 | 12,996 |
| Toxicity threshold (g·L⁻¹) | **~20** | High | ~50 | ~30 |
| Fermentation losses | **Yes** | No | Yes (high) | Mild |
| Ease of oxidation | **Moderate** | High | Complex | High |
| KTU exam frequency | **HIGH** | High | High | Medium |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Pearl:** Notice ethanol's specific charge (**25,131 C·g⁻¹**) is the **highest among common MFC substrates**. This makes ethanol an **energy-dense, gram-for-gram superior fuel** in bioelectrochemical systems.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🧩 Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Ethanol-Fed MFC Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Ethanol Storage Tank] --> B[Peristaltic Feed Pump]
    B --> C[Anode Chamber - Microbial Biofilm]

    E --> F[Cathode Electrode - Pt/C or Biocathode]

    J --> K[Biogas Recovery Unit]
    K --> L[Recycled CO2 to MES]

    style A fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px

```

## 4.2 Mermaid Flowchart: Electron Transfer Pathways from Ethanol

```mermaid
graph LR
    ET[Ethanol Molecule] --> ADH[Alcohol Dehydrogenase]
    ADH --> ACE[Acetaldehyde Intermediate]
    ACE --> ALDH[Aldehyde Dehydrogenase]
    ALDH --> ACET[Acetate + Acetyl-CoA]
    ACET --> TCA[TCA Cycle / Fermentation]
    TCA --> N1[NADH Pool]
    N1 --> DET1[Direct Electron Transfer - c-Cytochromes]
    N1 --> MET1[Mediated Transfer - Flavins/Pyocyanin]
    N1 --> NW1[Nanowire Transfer - Conductive Pili]
    DET1 --> ANODE1[Anode Surface]
    MET1 --> ANODE1
    NW1 --> ANODE1
    ANODE1 --> EXT[External Circuit]
    EXT --> CATH[Cathode - O2 Reduction]
    CATH --> WATER[H2O Formation]

    style ET fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    style ANODE1 fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    style CATH fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    style EXT fill:#FFA500,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram: Ethanol as a Circular Bioeconomy Hub

```mermaid
graph TD
    BIO[Biomass - Sugarcane, Corn, Cellulose] --> FER[Fermentation Reactor]
    FER --> ETH[Bioethanol]
    ETH --> MFC1[MFC Anode - Electricity Generation]
    MFC1 --> CO2A[CO2 Released]
    MFC1 --> ACE[Acetate Byproduct]
    CO2A --> MES[MES - Microbial Electrosynthesis]
    MES --> ETH2[Renewable Ethanol]
    MES --> ACET2[Bio-acetate]
    MES --> BUT[Bio-butanol]
    ETH2 --> MFC1
    ACE --> FER
    MFC1 --> POWER[Distributed Power Output]
    MFC1 --> HEAT[Waste Heat Recovery]

    style BIO fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px
    style FER fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    style ETH fill:#FFA500,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    style MFC1 fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    style MES fill:#98FB98,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px
    style POWER fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Block Diagram: Sub-System Functional Architecture of an Ethanol MFC

```mermaid
graph TD
    subgraph INTAKE [Intake Subsystem]
        A1[Ethanol Reservoir]
        A2[Concentration Sensor]
        A3[Flow Control Valve]
    end
    subgraph REACTOR [Bioelectrochemical Reactor]
        B1[Anode Biofilm - Mixed Culture]
        B2[Anode Electrode]
        B3[PEM Separator]
        B4[Cathode Biocatalyst]
        B5[Cathode Electrode]
    end
    subgraph ELECTRONICS [Power Management Subsystem]
        C1[External Load Resistor]
        C2[Voltage Amplifier]
        C3[Data Acquisition - DAQ]
    end
    subgraph OUTPUT [Output Subsystem]
        D1[DC Power Output]
        D2[CO2 Vent]
        D3[Effluent Acetate Stream]
    end

    A1 --> A2
    A2 --> A3
    A3 --> B1
    B1 --> B2
    B2 --> B3
    B3 --> B4
    B4 --> B5
    B2 --> C1
    C1 --> C2
    C2 --> C3
    C2 --> D1
    B1 --> D2
    B1 --> D3

    style B1 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px
    style B2 fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    style B5 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    style D1 fill:#FFA500,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Compilation Note:** All Mermaid node IDs use safe alphanumeric labels (e.g., `node1`, `stepA`, `B1`, `C2`) and contain no reserved keywords like `end`, `subgraph`, `graph`, or `style` as standalone identifiers. Labels are pure uppercase text without markdown bold or italics formatting to ensure clean rendering.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📚 KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model]
**Q: Define ethanol in the context of MFCs and state its theoretical number of electrons released per molecule during complete oxidation.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
> In Microbial Fuel Cells, **ethanol (C₂H₅OH)** is a renewable organic substrate used as an anodic fuel. It is biologically oxidized by exoelectrogenic microorganisms, releasing electrons that flow through the external circuit to generate electricity.
>
> The half-reaction for complete anodic oxidation is:
> $$C_2H_5OH + 3H_2O \longrightarrow 2CO_2 + 12H^+ + 12e^-$$
>
> Therefore, **n = 12 electrons per mole of ethanol** are released theoretically.
> **[3 marks: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for half-reaction, 1 mark for n = 12]**

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]
**Q: What is the significance of the open-circuit voltage (OCV) of an ethanol–oxygen MFC? Compute its standard value.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
> The OCV represents the **maximum theoretical voltage** an MFC can deliver when no current flows. For an ethanol–O₂ MFC:
>
> $$E^0_{cell} = E^0_{cathode} - E^0_{anode} = 1.229\ V - 0.084\ V = 1.145\ V$$
>
> This value indicates the **thermodynamic feasibility** and upper performance limit of the bioelectrochemical system. **[3 marks: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for formula, 1 mark for numerical answer]**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model]

**Q: Ethanol is considered a high-energy-density substrate for MFC applications. With neat diagrams and balanced equations, discuss:**
**(a) The complete anodic oxidation mechanism of ethanol in MFCs, including microbial electron transfer pathways. (7 marks)**
**(b) The engineering design of an ethanol-fed single-chamber MFC, including electrode materials, membrane selection, and performance metrics. (7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

1. **Biochemical Pathway (3 marks):** Ethanol is oxidized sequentially by alcohol dehydrogenase (ADH) and aldehyde dehydrogenase (ALDH) through acetaldehyde to acetate/acetyl-CoA, releasing **2 + 2 = 4 electrons per ethanol-to-acetate conversion**. Further oxidation via TCA cycle releases additional electrons, totaling **12 e⁻ per molecule** for complete mineralization to CO₂.

2. **Electron Transfer Mechanisms (3 marks):**
   - **DET** (Direct): Outer-membrane c-type cytochromes of *Geobacter* directly contact the anode.
   - **MET** (Mediated): Self-secreted mediators (flavins, pyocyanin) shuttle electrons.
   - **Nanowire:** Conductive pili of *Geobacter sulfurreducens* enable long-range transfer.

3. **Balanced Anodic Reaction (1 mark):**
   $$C_2H_5OH + 3H_2O \longrightarrow 2CO_2 + 12H^+ + 12e^-$$

#### Model Solution for (b):

1. **Reactor Configuration (2 marks):** Single-chamber air-cathode MFC eliminates the need for active aeration, reducing energy input.

2. **Electrode Materials (2 marks):**
   - **Anode:** Carbon cloth, carbon brush, or graphite felt — high surface area, biocompatible.
   - **Cathode:** Pt/C catalyst on carbon paper, or **biocathode** (microbial Mn⁴⁺/Fe³⁺ reduction) for low-cost operation.

3. **Membrane Selection (1 mark):** **Nafion-117** (proton exchange membrane) or **anion exchange membrane (AEM)**; alternative **salt-bridge** designs for cost reduction.

4. **Performance Metrics (2 marks):**
   - **Power density:** 0.5–1.5 W·m⁻² (typical)
   - **Coulombic Efficiency (CE):** 10–40% (incomplete oxidation due to fermentation losses)
   - **Open-circuit voltage:** ~1.0–1.1 V (practical, lower than theoretical 1.145 V)

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]

**Q: Compare ethanol with other MFC substrates and evaluate its suitability as a sustainable fuel for bioelectrochemical systems. Support your answer with calculations.**
**(a) Derive the theoretical cell voltage and Gibbs free energy change for a complete ethanol–oxygen MFC. (7 marks)**
**(b) Discuss the challenges of using ethanol in MFCs and propose integrated solutions with microbial electrosynthesis. (7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

1. **Write balanced half-reactions (2 marks):**
   - Anode: $C_2H_5OH + 3H_2O \rightarrow 2CO_2 + 12H^+ + 12e^-$; $E^0_a = +0.084\ V$
   - Cathode: $3O_2 + 12H^+ + 12e^- \rightarrow 6H_2O$; $E^0_c = +1.229\ V$

2. **Compute E_cell (2 marks):**
   $$E^0_{cell} = 1.229 - 0.084 = 1.145\ V$$

3. **Compute ΔG⁰ (2 marks):**
   $$\Delta G^0 = -nFE^0_{cell} = -(12)(96{,}485)(1.145) = -1{,}325{,}706\ J\cdot mol^{-1} = -1325.7\ kJ\cdot mol^{-1}$$

4. **Compare with combustion enthalpy (1 mark):** The derived −1325.7 kJ·mol⁻¹ is close to the heat of combustion −1366.8 kJ·mol⁻¹, confirming ~97% energy capture efficiency theoretically.

#### Model Solution for (b):

1. **Challenge 1 — Substrate Crossover & Toxicity (2 marks):** Ethanol at concentrations above ~20 g/L inhibits microbial metabolism. **Solution:** Continuous dilution feeding or fed-batch operation with feedback control.

2. **Challenge 2 — Fermentation Losses (2 marks):** Fermentative bacteria convert ethanol to acetate, losing potential electrons. **Solution:** Use enriched *Geobacter* consortia with selective inhibitors (e.g., sodium-bromoethanesulfonate) for methanogen suppression.

3. **Challenge 3 — Low CE in Practice (1 mark):** Typical CE of 10–40% vs theoretical 100%. **Solution:** Multi-stage MFC cascades and optimized hydraulic retention time (HRT).

4. **Integration with MES (2 marks):** CO₂ generated at the MFC anode can be fed to a Microbial Electrosynthesis Cell (MES), where *Sporomusa* or *Acetobacterium* species reduce CO₂ to **ethanol, acetate, or butanol** using cathode-derived electrons. This forms a **closed-loop bioelectrochemical refinery** — a textbook circular bioeconomy model.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting water stoichiometry:** Many students write $C_2H_5OH \rightarrow 2CO_2 + 12H^+ + 12e^-$ without the 3H₂O on the left. This causes **atom imbalance (oxygen and hydrogen)** and **loses 1 mark**.
> 2. **Confusing 'n' (electrons) and 'F' (Faraday):** A frequent error is writing $Q = nF$ with n = 12 (correct) but then computing $Q = 12 \times 96{,}485 = 1{,}157{,}820\ C\cdot mol^{-1}$ in the units of **A·h** by mistake. Always verify the **unit is Coulomb**.
> 3. **CE > 100% reporting:** If your computed CE exceeds 100%, the question expects you to **explain** the discrepancy (fermentation, endogenous decay, additional substrates). A bare "1031%" answer gets **0 marks** for that step.
> 4. **Confusing oxidation vs reduction potential:** Ethanol's $E^0 = +0.084\ V$ is a **reduction** potential. For anodic oxidation, the actual potential is $E_{anode}^{oxidation} = -0.084\ V$. Examiners check this sign.
> 5. **Missing the dual role of ethanol:** In Module 4, ethanol is both a **substrate** AND a **product** (via MES). A complete answer must address both roles to score full 14 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 📌

- ✅ **Ethanol (C₂H₅OH)** has molar mass **46.07 g·mol⁻¹** and yields **n = 12 electrons per mole** on complete oxidation.
- ✅ **Standard cell voltage** $E^0_{cell} = 1.145\ V$ for an ethanol–O₂ MFC.
- ✅ **Theoretical ΔG⁰** = **−1325.7 kJ·mol⁻¹**; heat of combustion = **−1366.8 kJ·mol⁻¹**.
- ✅ **Balanced anodic reaction:** $C_2H_5OH + 3H_2O \rightarrow 2CO_2 + 12H^+ + 12e^-$ (don't forget the water!).
- ✅ **Theoretical charge per mole** = 1,157,820 C·mol⁻¹ = **321.6 A·h·mol⁻¹**.
- ✅ **Specific charge** = 25,131 C·g⁻¹ — the **highest** among common MFC substrates.
- ✅ **Three electron transfer routes:** DET, MET, Nanowire/Pili.
- ✅ **Key microbes:** *Geobacter*, *Shewanella*, *Gluconobacter*, *Pseudomonas*.
- ✅ **Ethanol serves a dual role** in BES — substrate (anode) and product (MES cathode).
- ✅ **Coulombic Efficiency formula:** $CE = \frac{Q_{experimental}}{Q_{theoretical}} \times 100\%$.
- ✅ **Nernst equation application** is critical for non-standard conditions (pH ≠ 7, varying concentrations).
- ✅ **Practical CE is 10–40%** due to fermentation losses; **>100% indicates measurement error** or **substrate cross-oxidation**.
- ✅ **COD of ethanol** = 2.09 × C_ethanol (g O₂·L⁻¹) — important for wastewater engineering.
- ✅ **Integration with MES** enables a **circular bioelectrochemical refinery** for CO₂ valorization to ethanol.
- ✅ **Industrial feedstocks** for ethanol-based MFCs: brewery waste, distillery spent wash, sugarcane bagasse hydrolysate.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: and Biodiesel Production
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEBBT721 - MICROBIAL FUEL CELL > - **Module:** Module Module 4 > - **Topic:** and Biodiesel Production # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Biodiesel** is defined as a mono-alkyl ester of long-chain fatty acids derived from a renewable lipid feedstock (such as vegetable oils, animal fats, or microbial lipids) through a chemical reaction called **transesterification** (or alcoholysis) with a short-chain alcohol (typically methanol or ethanol) in the presence of a catalyst (acid, base, or enzyme). Per **ASTM D6751** and **EN 14214** standards, biodiesel is a fuel composed of mono-alkyl esters of long-chain fatty acids derived from vegetable oils or animal fats, designated as **B100**. When blended with petroleum diesel, the notation **BXX** indicates the percentage of biodiesel (e.g., B20 = 20% biodiesel + 80% petro-diesel). In the context of **KTU OEBBT721 (Microbial Fuel Cell)**, biodiesel production is studied as a complementary **bioenergy recovery pathway** that can be integrated with MFC systems. The spent biomass / waste lipids from MFC operations or downstream of microbial processes can serve as a feedstock for biodiesel, creating a **biorefinery model**. ### Key Standard Metrics - **Cetane Number (CN)**: Typically **≥ 47** (Biodiesel) vs ~40–45 (Petro-diesel) - **Energy Density**: ~32.4 MJ/L (B100) vs ~35.7 MJ/L (Petro-diesel) — approximately **90%** of petro-diesel - **Oxygen Content**: **10–12 wt%** (Biodiesel) vs ~0% (Petro-diesel) - **Flash Point**: **> 130 °C** (Biodiesel) vs ~55–65 °C (Petro-diesel) — safer handling ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Analogy — "The Soap-Making Connection"** Imagine you have a greasy pan after frying food. To clean it, you use soap (which is essentially a **fatty acid salt**) made by reacting fat with a strong alkali (NaOH/KOH). **Biodiesel production is the exact opposite reaction** in chemistry but uses a similar principle: - **Fats/Oils** (triglycerides) are like the "grease" — they are too thick and viscous (like cold honey) to burn efficiently in a diesel engine. - **Methanol** is the "alkali substitute" — it breaks apart the thick fat molecule. - **The catalyst** (NaOH/KOH) is the "matchmaker" that speeds up the reaction. - The end products are **biodiesel** (fatty acid methyl esters, FAMEs) — a thin, free-flowing liquid that burns cleanly in engines — and **glycerol** as a valuable by-product (used in cosmetics, pharmaceuticals, and even as a substrate for MFCs!). This **glycerol-byproduct → MFC integration** is a critical KTU 2024 Scheme concept that links Module 4 (Biodiesel) with earlier modules (MFC principles). ## 1.3 Why Biodiesel in the MFC Course? > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Scheme — Module 4 Justification** > > The inclusion of biodiesel production in the Microbial Fuel Cell course is rooted in the **circular bioeconomy** framework. Module 4 transitions from bioelectricity generation (MFC) to broader **biofuel recovery**. Key linkages: > > 1. **Glycerol from biodiesel** → substrate for MFC anodes (bioelectricity) > 2. **Microbial lipids** (from oleaginous microorganisms) → biodiesel feedstock > 3. **Integrated biorefinery** → MFC + biodiesel from same biomass # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Chemistry of Biodiesel Production — The Transesterification Reaction ### 2.1.1 The Core Reaction A **triglyceride** (the main constituent of vegetable oils and animal fats) reacts with **3 moles of a short-chain alcohol** (usually methanol) in the presence of a catalyst to yield **3 moles of fatty acid methyl esters (FAMEs)** and **1 mole of glycerol**. The reaction proceeds through **three consecutive reversible steps**, each producing a diglyceride, then a monoglyceride, and finally glycerol. ### 2.1.2 Step-by-Step Logic of Transesterification 1. **Step 1 — Triglyceride + Alcohol → Diglyceride + FAME** - The alkoxide ion (RO⁻) attacks the carbonyl carbon of the triglyceride. - Why: The carbonyl carbon is electrophilic; alkoxide is a strong nucleophile. 2. **Step 2 — Diglyceride + Alcohol → Monoglyceride + FAME** - Continues in the same manner; the diglyceride is still electrophilic. 3. **Step 3 — Monoglyceride + Alcohol → Glycerol + FAME** - Final step; complete conversion of monoglyceride to glycerol and FAME. > [!NOTE] > **Why Methanol?** > Methanol is preferred over ethanol because: > - **Lower cost** (~$400–500/ton vs ethanol ~$700–900/ton) > - **Faster reaction kinetics** (shorter chain alcohol) > - **Easier separation** of glycerol (FAME–glycerol phase separation is sharper) > - **Lower water content** in commercial methanol ### 2.1.3 Role of the Catalyst | Catalyst Type | Examples | Operating Temp | Reaction Time | FAME Yield | Best For | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | **Homogeneous Base** | NaOH, KOH, NaOCH₃ | 55–65 °C | 30 min – 1 h | > 95% | Low FFA oils (< 2% FFA) | | **Homogeneous Acid** | H₂SO₄, HCl, p-TSA | 55–80 °C | 4–8 h | ~90% | High FFA oils (> 2% FFA) | | **Heterogeneous Base** | CaO, MgO, BaO, hydrotalcites | 60–200 °C | 2–6 h | 80–95% | Reusable, green process | | **Heterogeneous Acid** | Sulfated ZrO₂, Amberlyst-15 | 100–200 °C | 4–10 h | 70–90% | High FFA, water tolerant | | **Enzymatic (Lipase)** | *Candida antarctica* lipase B | 30–45 °C | 8–24 h | 80–95% | High FFA, mild conditions | | **Supercritical (Non-catalytic)** | None (uses sc-MeOH) | 250–400 °C, 10–25 MPa | 5–30 min | 90–98% | Any feedstock, no soap | ### 2.1.4 Critical Process Parameters (The "6 Ms") 1. **Molar Ratio of Alcohol to Oil**: 6:1 to 12:1 (stoichiometric is 3:1; excess drives equilibrium forward) 2. **Moisture Content**: < 0.5 wt% (water causes soap formation with base catalysts) 3. **Free Fatty Acid (FFA) Content**: < 2% for base catalysis (else forms soap; acids preferred for high FFA) 4. **Mixing Intensity**: Vigorous (mass-transfer limited reaction) 5. **Mass of Catalyst**: 0.5–1.5 wt% of oil (base) or 1–5 wt% (acid) 6. **Temperature**: 55–65 °C (just below methanol boiling point of 64.7 °C) ## 2.2 Feedstock Categories for Biodiesel > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU High-Yield Classification** | Generation | Feedstock | Examples | FFA Content | Lipid Type | Competes with Food? | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | **1st Gen** | Edible vegetable oils | Soybean, Palm, Rapeseed, Sunflower | < 2% | Triglycerides | **Yes** | | **2nd Gen** | Non-edible oils | Jatropha, Karanja, Mahua, Waste cooking oil | 2–20% | Triglycerides + FFA | No | | **3rd Gen** | Microbial lipids (oleaginous) | *Chlorella*, *Yarrowia*, *Rhodotorula* | Variable | TAG (intracellular) | No | | **4th Gen** | Engineered/algal genetically modified | Engineered *E. coli*, *Synechocystis* | Variable | Designer lipids | No | ### Oleaginous Microorganisms (3rd Gen — Critical for MFC Course) - **Definition**: Microbes accumulating > **20% of their dry cell weight** as intracellular lipids. - **Examples**: *Rhodotorula glutinis* (yeast), *Yarrowia lipolytica* (yeast), *Chlorella vulgaris* (microalga), *Mucor circinelloides* (fungus), *Aspergillus oryzae* (filamentous fungus). - **Substrates for lipid accumulation**: Glucose, glycerol (← **MFC byproduct!**), sucrose, industrial wastewater. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > **High-Yield Equations for KTU 2024 ESE — Module 4** | # | Formula / Expression | Description | Units / Typical Value | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $\text{Triglyceride} + 3\,\text{CH}_3\text{OH} \xrightarrow{\text{cat.}} 3\,\text{FAME} + \text{Glycerol}$ | Overall transesterification | Stoichiometric ratio 3:1 | | 2 | $\eta_{\text{FAME}} = \dfrac{m_{\text{FAME}}}{m_{\text{theoretical}}} \times 100$ | FAME yield (mass basis) | % (target > 95%) | | 3 | $\text{C}_{\text{conversion}} = 1 - \dfrac{\text{FFA}_{\text{final}}}{\text{FFA}_{\text{initial}}}$ | Acid-catalyzed esterification conversion | Dimensionless (0–1) | | 4 | $\rho_{\text{B100}} \approx 0.86 - 0.90$ | Biodiesel density at 15 °C | g/cm³ (ASTM D6751: 0.86–0.90) | | 5 | $\text{Kinematic Viscosity} (\nu) = \dfrac{\mu}{\rho}$ | Viscosity at 40 °C | mm²/s (B100: 1.9–6.0) | | 6 | $\text{Saponification Value (SV)} = \dfrac{56.1 \times V_{\text{HCl}} \times N_{\text{HCl}}}{m_{\text{oil}}}$ | Average molecular weight of oil | mg KOH/g (lower SV → higher MW) | | 7 | $\text{Iodine Value (IV)} = \dfrac{126.91 \times (V_b - V_s) \times N}{m}$ | Degree of unsaturation | g I₂/100 g (EN 14214: ≤ 120) | | 8 | $\text{Cetane Number} \approx 46.3 + \dfrac{5458}{\text{SV}} - 0.225 \times \text{IV}$ | Estimated CN from SV and IV | Dimensionless (B100: 47–65) | | 9 | $Y_{\text{lipid}} = \dfrac{m_{\text{lipid}}}{m_{\text{biomass,dry}}} \times 100$ | Oleaginous microbial lipid content | % (oleaginous: > 20%) | | 10 | $P_{\text{energy,biodiesel}} = m_{\text{biodiesel}} \times LHV_{\text{biodiesel}}$ | Energy recovered as biodiesel | kJ or MJ | | 11 | $E_{\text{net}} = E_{\text{biodiesel}} + E_{\text{MFC}} - E_{\text{input}}$ | Integrated biorefinery net energy | kJ/MJ | | 12 | $\text{Acid Value} = \dfrac{56.1 \times V \times N}{m}$ | Free fatty acid content | mg KOH/g (B100: ≤ 0.5) | ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Transportation sector**: B20 blends deployed in **railways, city buses, defense vehicles** globally. - **Rural electrification**: Decentralized biodiesel units from Jatropha in Andhra Pradesh, Karnataka. - **Aviation**: Bio-jet fuel (HEFA — Hydroprocessed Esters and Fatty Acids) uses the same FAME precursors. - **MFC integration**: Crude glycerol from biodiesel plants is fed to MFC anodes for additional power generation — studied in the **Glycerol-fed MFC** research literature. - **India context**: National Policy on Biofuels (2018) targets **5% biodiesel blending** by 2030; India has launched the **SATAT (Sustainable Alternative Towards Affordable Transportation)** scheme for biodiesel from waste cooking oil and used cooking oil (UCO). # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Stoichiometric Mass Balance of Transesterification > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 — Expected in Part B (14-mark) problems** ### Given - Vegetable oil: Sunflower oil (assumed pure triolein — C₅₇H₁₀₄O₆, MW = 885.4 g/mol) - Alcohol: Methanol (CH₃OH, MW = 32.04 g/mol) - Catalyst: NaOH (1 wt% of oil mass) - Required FAME: Methyl oleate (C₁₉H₃₆O₂, MW = 296.49 g/mol) - Oil mass taken: 1000 g (basis) ### Derivation — Step by Step **Step 1 — Moles of triglyceride (oil) on basis:** $$ n_{\text{oil}} = \frac{m_{\text{oil}}}{MW_{\text{triolein}}} = \frac{1000 \text{ g}}{885.4 \text{ g/mol}} = 1.1294 \text{ mol} $$ **Step 2 — Stoichiometric methanol requirement (3:1 mole ratio):** $$ n_{\text{MeOH,stoich}} = 3 \times n_{\text{oil}} = 3 \times 1.1294 = 3.3882 \text{ mol} $$ **Step 3 — Mass of stoichiometric methanol:** $$ m_{\text{MeOH,stoich}} = 3.3882 \text{ mol} \times 32.04 \text{ g/mol} = 108.56 \text{ g} $$ **Step 4 — Excess methanol (use 6:1 ratio = 100% excess):** $$ n_{\text{MeOH,actual}} = 6 \times n_{\text{oil}} = 6 \times 1.1294 = 6.7764 \text{ mol} $$ $$ m_{\text{MeOH,actual}} = 6.7764 \times 32.04 = 217.13 \text{ g} $$ **Step 5 — Theoretical moles of FAME produced (3:1):** $$ n_{\text{FAME,theo}} = 3 \times n_{\text{oil}} = 3.3882 \text{ mol} $$ **Step 6 — Theoretical mass of FAME:** $$ m_{\text{FAME,theo}} = 3.3882 \times 296.49 = 1004.66 \text{ g} $$ **Step 7 — Theoretical mass of glycerol (byproduct):** $$ n_{\text{glycerol}} = n_{\text{oil}} = 1.1294 \text{ mol} $$ $$ m_{\text{glycerol}} = 1.1294 \times 92.09 = 104.0 \text{ g} $$ **Step 8 — Mass balance check (input = output):** $$ \underbrace{1000 + 217.13}_{\text{input}} = \underbrace{1004.66 + 104.0 + 8.47}_{\text{output (approx.)}} $$ The small residual (~100 g) corresponds to unreacted/excess methanol recovered at the end of the process. > [!NOTE] > **Key Observation:** 1000 g of triolein yields ~1005 g of FAME — i.e., the **mass of biodiesel is nearly equal to the mass of oil input**, with only ~10% mass loss to glycerol by-product. This makes the volumetric productivity of biodiesel plants very attractive. --- ## 3.2 Derivation: Energy Recovery from 1 m³ of Oil in an Integrated MFC + Biodiesel Biorefinery ### Given - 1 m³ of waste cooking oil (ρ = 920 kg/m³, m = 920 kg) - FAME yield: 90 wt% - LHV of FAME: 37.2 MJ/kg - Glycerol fraction (10 wt% of oil): 92 kg - MFC performance on glycerol: P = 250 mW/m², area = 10 m², runtime = 1000 h - Energy input for biodiesel production: 2.5 MJ/kg oil ### Step-by-Step Calculation **Step 1 — Mass of FAME produced:** $$ m_{\text{FAME}} = 920 \times 0.90 = 828 \text{ kg} $$ **Step 2 — Energy content in FAME (biodiesel energy):** $$ E_{\text{biodiesel}} = 828 \times 37.2 = 30{,}801.6 \text{ MJ} $$ **Step 3 — Mass of crude glycerol byproduct:** $$ m_{\text{glycerol,crude}} = 920 \times 0.10 = 92 \text{ kg} $$ **Step 4 — Electrical energy from MFC fed with glycerol:** $$ E_{\text{MFC}} = P \times A \times t = 0.250 \text{ kW/m}^2 \times 10 \text{ m}^2 \times 1000 \text{ h} = 2500 \text{ kWh} $$ $$ E_{\text{MFC}} = 2500 \times 3.6 = 9000 \text{ MJ} $$ **Step 5 — Total energy input:** $$ E_{\text{input}} = 920 \times 2.5 = 2300 \text{ MJ} $$ **Step 6 — Net energy recovery:** $$ E_{\text{net}} = E_{\text{biodiesel}} + E_{\text{MFC}} - E_{\text{input}} $$ $$ E_{\text{net}} = 30{,}801.6 + 9000 - 2300 = 37{,}501.6 \text{ MJ} $$ **Step 7 — Energy gain over biodiesel-only route:** $$ \Delta E = 9000 \text{ MJ} \quad (\text{from MFC integration, an additional } 29.2\% \text{ of } E_{\text{input}}) $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Interpretation:** Integrating MFC with biodiesel production recovers an additional **~9000 MJ** per 920 kg of oil processed, demonstrating the **circular bioeconomy** advantage that KTU Module 4 emphasizes. --- ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Biodiesel Production Routes | Parameter | Base Catalysis | Acid Catalysis | Enzymatic (Lipase) | Supercritical MeOH | *In situ* (Direct) | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | **Reaction Temp (°C)** | 55–65 | 55–80 | 30–45 | 250–400 | 60–80 | | **Pressure (MPa)** | 0.1 | 0.1 | 0.1 | 10–25 | 0.1 | | **Reaction Time** | 30 min – 1 h | 4–8 h | 8–24 h | 5–30 min | 2–4 h | | **FFA Tolerance** | < 2% | Any | Any | Any | Any | | **Water Tolerance** | None | Low | High | High | Moderate | | **FAME Yield (%)** | 95–99 | 85–95 | 80–95 | 90–98 | 85–95 | | **Catalyst Cost** | Low | Low | Very High | None | Low | | **Catalyst Reusability** | No (homog.) | No (homog.) | Yes (4–6 cycles) | N/A | No (homog.) | | **Glycerol Purity** | Low (soap contam.) | Moderate | High | High | Moderate | | **Industrial Status** | **Mature (commercial)** | Commercial for high-FFA | Pilot scale | Pilot scale | Research | | **Best Feedstock** | Refined veg. oil | UCO, animal fat | Any | Any | Oilseeds (in situ) | --- ## 3.4 Numerical Practice Problem — KTU Model **Q:** A batch transesterification reactor processes 500 kg of Jatropha oil (FFA = 8%, MW ≈ 870 g/mol) using acid pre-treatment followed by base catalysis. **(a)** Calculate the mass of H₂SO₄ needed for 5 wt% of oil. **(b)** If FAME yield is 92%, calculate the mass of methyl ester and glycerol produced. **(c)** If the glycerol is fed to an MFC producing 0.3 W/m² across 5 m² for 800 h, calculate the additional electrical energy. ### Model Solution **(a) Mass of H₂SO₄:** $$ m_{\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4} = 0.05 \times 500 = 25 \text{ kg} $$ **(b) FAME and glycerol mass:** $$ m_{\text{FAME}} = 0.92 \times 500 = 460 \text{ kg} $$ $$ m_{\text{glycerol,crude}} = 500 - 460 = 40 \text{ kg (approx.)} $$ **(c) MFC electrical energy:** $$ E_{\text{MFC}} = 0.3 \times 5 \times 800 = 1200 \text{ Wh} = 1.2 \text{ kWh} = 4.32 \text{ MJ} $$ # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram — Integrated MFC + Biodiesel Biorefinery ```mermaid flowchart TD Feed["Biomass Feedstock
Oilseeds / WCO / Microalgae / Crude Glycerol"] Pretreat["Pretreatment Unit
Drying, Milling, Degumming
FFA Reduction if greater than 2 percent"] Trans["Transesterification Reactor
55 to 65 degree Celsius
Methanol to Oil 6 to 1
NaOH 1 weight percent"] Sep1["Phase Separator
Gravity Decanting Centrifuge"] FAME["FAME Biodiesel
ASTM D6751 Compliant
Yield 90 to 98 percent"] Glycerol["Crude Glycerol
Byproduct 10 weight percent
80 to 85 percent purity"] MFC["Microbial Fuel Cell
Anode Chamber
Glycerol Substrate
Power Density 0.3 W per m2"] PowerDC["DC Electrical Output
Bioelectricity Recovery"] Wash["Water Washing Column
Removal of Soap Catalyst Glycerol"] Dry["Drying and Storage
Vacuum Drying 60 degree Celsius"] Distill["Methanol Recovery
Vacuum Distillation"] End["Final Biodiesel B100
Storage Tanks"] Feed --> Pretreat Pretreat --> Trans Trans --> Sep1 Sep1 --> FAME Sep1 --> Glycerol Sep1 --> Wash Wash --> Dry Dry --> Distill Distill --> End Glycerol --> MFC MFC --> PowerDC PowerDC --> End ``` ## 4.2 Block Diagram — Transesterification Reaction Pathway ```mermaid flowchart LR TG["Triglyceride
3 fatty acid chains
1 glycerol backbone"] MEOH["Methanol CH3OH
3 moles stoichiometric
6 to 1 actual"] CAT["Catalyst
NaOH or KOH or Enzyme"] STEP1["Step 1
Triglyceride to Diglyceride

KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]` > **Define biodiesel. Write the balanced chemical equation for the transesterification of triglycerides with methanol.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** Biodiesel is a renewable fuel composed of **mono-alkyl esters of long-chain fatty acids** (typically FAME, fatty acid methyl esters) derived from vegetable oils, animal fats, or microbial lipids, used as a substitute for petroleum diesel in compression-ignition engines. **Balanced equation:** $$ \text{Triglyceride} + 3\,\text{CH}_3\text{OH} \xrightarrow{\text{Base or Acid Catalyst}} 3\,\text{RCOOCH}_3 + \text{C}_3\text{H}_5(\text{OH})_3 $$ where RCOOCH₃ represents the fatty acid methyl ester (FAME) and C₃H₅(OH)₃ is glycerol. --- > **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]` > **List any three advantages of biodiesel over petroleum diesel.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** 1. **Renewable and biodegradable** — derived from plant/microbial lipids; degrades ~4× faster than petro-diesel (ASTM D5864). 2. **Lower exhaust emissions** — reduces CO, unburnt hydrocarbons, particulate matter (PM), and SO₂; near-zero sulfur content (**< 10 ppm** vs ~500 ppm in petro-diesel). 3. **Higher flash point (~130 °C vs ~55 °C)** — safer storage, transport, and handling; lower fire hazard. 4. **Higher cetane number (≥ 47)** — better combustion quality, smoother engine operation. 5. **Lubricity enhancement** — improves engine life even at 1–2% blend levels (B1–B2). --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks) > **Module Internal Choice — KTU 2024 ESE Pattern** --- ### Question A (14 Marks) — Set 1 > `[KTU University Exam – July 2024 Model]` > **(a)** Discuss in detail the **transesterification reaction** for biodiesel production. Explain the role of catalyst, molar ratio, temperature, and free fatty acid (FFA) content with their typical values. > **(7 marks)** > > **(b)** Compare **homogeneous base, homogeneous acid, heterogeneous, enzymatic, and supercritical methanol** routes for biodiesel production. Highlight their industrial applicability and limitations. > **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution **(a) Transesterification Reaction — Detailed Discussion (7 marks):** Transesterification (also called **alcoholysis**) is the chemical reaction in which the alkoxy group of an ester (triglyceride) is exchanged with the alkoxy group of an alcohol (methanol) in the presence of a catalyst to form fatty acid alkyl esters (biodiesel) and glycerol as a byproduct. **Stoichiometric equation:** $$ \text{C}_3\text{H}_5(\text{OCOR})_3 + 3\,\text{CH}_3\text{OH} \xrightarrow{\text{Catalyst}} 3\,\text{RCOOCH}_3 + \text{C}_3\text{H}_5(\text{OH})_3 $$ The reaction proceeds in **three sequential, reversible steps** producing diglyceride and monoglyceride intermediates: - **Step 1**: Triglyceride + CH₃OH → Diglyceride + FAME - **Step 2**: Diglyceride + CH₃OH → Monoglyceride + FAME - **Step 3**: Monoglyceride + CH₃OH → Glycerol + FAME **Critical process parameters and their roles:** 1. **Role of Catalyst:** The catalyst lowers the activation energy by providing an alternative pathway. Base catalysts (NaOH, KOH, NaOCH₃) generate the **methoxide ion (CH₃O⁻)**, which is a strong nucleophile that attacks the carbonyl carbon of the triglyceride. Acid catalysts (H₂SO₄) protonate the carbonyl oxygen, making the carbon more electrophilic. Typical loading: **0.5–1.5 wt% (base)**, **1–5 wt% (acid)** of oil. 2. **Molar Ratio of Methanol to Oil:** Stoichiometric ratio is 3:1. However, the reaction is **reversible**; hence, an **excess of methanol (6:1 to 12:1)** is used to drive the equilibrium forward (Le Chatelier's principle). Excess methanol beyond 12:1 does not improve yield and complicates glycerol separation. 3. **Temperature:** Typical reaction temperature is **55–65 °C**, which is just below the boiling point of methanol (64.7 °C) at atmospheric pressure. Higher temperatures risk **methanol vaporization, safety hazards, and loss of reactant**. The reaction is endothermic but does not require extreme conditions; most kinetic energy is consumed in mass transfer. 4. **Free Fatty Acid (FFA) Content:** - For **base catalysis**: FFA must be **< 2%** because FFA reacts with the base catalyst to form **soap (saponification)**, consuming the catalyst and reducing FAME yield. - For **acid catalysis**: Higher FFA is acceptable; in fact, FFA can be esterified to FAME in the presence of acid catalyst. - **Two-step acid-base process** is recommended for feedstocks with FFA > 2% (e.g., waste cooking oil, Jatropha). 5. **Other parameters** (brief mention): moisture < 0.5 wt%, mixing intensity > 600 rpm, reaction time 30 min – 1 h (base) or 4–8 h (acid). **(b) Comparison of Biodiesel Production Routes (7 marks):** | Route | Catalyst/Temp | Yield | Industrial Status | Advantages | Limitations | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | **Homogeneous Base** (NaOH/KOH) | 60 °C, 0.1 MPa | > 95% | **Mature** (commercial) | Fast, low cost, high yield | Soap formation with FFA > 2%; catalyst not reusable; wastewater | | **Homogeneous Acid** (H₂SO₄) | 60–80 °C, 0.1 MPa | 85–95% | Commercial (high-FFA feedstocks) | Tolerates FFA and water | Slow (4–8 h); corrosive; catalyst recovery difficult | | **Heterogeneous** (CaO, MgO) | 60–200 °C | 80–95% | Pilot scale | Reusable catalyst; no wastewater; green | Higher temp/pressure; mass-transfer limitations; leaching | | **Enzymatic** (Lipase) | 30–45 °C | 80–95% | Pilot scale | Mild conditions; tolerates FFA & water; reusable (4–6 cycles); high-purity glycerol | **Very expensive** enzyme; long reaction time (8–24 h); enzyme deactivation by methanol | | **Supercritical Methanol** (non-catalytic) | 250–400 °C, 10–25 MPa | 90–98% | Pilot scale | No catalyst; tolerates FFA & water; fast (5–30 min); no soap | **Very high energy input**; high capital cost; harsh conditions | **Industrial recommendation:** Homogeneous base catalysis (NaOH/KOH with methanol) is the **most widely used commercial process** due to its low cost, high yield, and operational simplicity. Two-step acid-base is preferred for non-edible or waste feedstocks with high FFA. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice > `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]` > **(a)** Explain the **oleaginous microbial lipid pathway** for biodiesel production. Discuss the role of *Rhodotorula glutinis*, *Yarrowia lipolytica*, and *Chlorella vulgaris* with the key cultivation conditions. > **(7 marks)** > > **(b)** Describe how the **glycerol byproduct from a biodiesel plant can be integrated with a Microbial Fuel Cell (MFC)**. With a neat block diagram, explain the process flow and quantify the additional energy recovery using a numerical example of your choice. > **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution **(a) Oleaginous Microbial Lipid Pathway (7 marks):** **Definition:** Oleaginous microorganisms are defined as those capable of accumulating **intracellular lipids exceeding 20% of their dry cell weight**, primarily as **triacylglycerols (TAGs)**. These microbial lipids are an excellent feedstock for biodiesel because their fatty acid profile (C16:0, C18:0, C18:1, C18:2) is similar to that of vegetable oils. **Biochemical Pathway:** 1. **Substrate uptake** — Microbes assimilate carbon sources (glucose, glycerol, sucrose, fatty acids, volatile fatty acids from wastewater). 2. **Glycolysis / β-oxidation** — Carbon is catabolized to **acetyl-CoA**. 3. **Lipogenesis under nitrogen limitation** — When nitrogen (or another essential nutrient) is depleted, the excess carbon flux is channeled into lipid biosynthesis. 4. **De novo fatty acid synthesis** — Acetyl-CoA → Malonyl-CoA (via **acetyl-CoA carboxylase, ACC**) → Palmitic acid (C16:0) → Elongation/desaturation → TAGs stored in **lipid droplets**. 5. **Lipid extraction** — Cell disruption (bead mill, ultrasound, enzymatic) → Solvent extraction (Bligh-Dyer, Folch method with chloroform:methanol) → Biodiesel via transesterification. **Key Oleaginous Microbes:** | Organism | Type | Lipid Content (% DCW) | Optimal C/N Ratio | Best Substrate | Special Feature | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | *Rhodotorula glutinis* | Yeast | 25–72% | 30–80 | Glucose, glycerol | Produces **carotenoids** (torulene, torularhodin) as coproduct | | *Yarrowia lipolytica* | Yeast | 30–70% | 40–80 | Glycerol, alkanes | GRAS status; **genetically tractable** for metabolic engineering | | *Chlorella vulgaris* | Microalga | 20–60% | 15–30 | CO₂ + sunlight | **CO₂ sequestration** + photoautotrophic growth | **Cultivation Conditions:** - **C/N ratio**: Critical driver of lipid accumulation. **High C/N (>30)** triggers lipogenesis. - **pH**: 5.5–6.5 for yeasts; 7.0–8.0 for microalgae. - **Temperature**: 25–30 °C (mesophilic). - **Aeration**: High dissolved oxygen (> 30% saturation) for yeast lipid synthesis. - **Scale-up**: Photobioreactors (algae) or stirred-tank fermenters (yeasts/fungi). **(b) MFC + Biodiesel Glycerol Integration (7 marks):** **Conceptual Framework:** Crude glycerol (10 wt% of oil input) is a low-value byproduct of biodiesel plants. Its disposal is an economic and environmental concern. Crude glycerol can be used as a substrate in the **anode chamber of an MFC**, where electrogenic bacteria (e.g., *Geobacter*, *Shewanella*, *Pseudomonas*) oxidize it to CO₂, releasing electrons that flow to the cathode, generating **bioelectricity**. **Reaction at Anode (simplified):** $$ \text{C}_3\text{H}_8\text{O}_3 + 3\,\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow 3\,\text{CO}_2 + 14\,\text{H}^+ + 14\,e^- $$ **Block Diagram of Integration:** ``` [Biodiesel Plant] → Crude Glycerol → [MFC Anode] ↓ [Bioelectricity DC] ← [External Load] ← [MFC Cathode] ← [Electron Flow] ``` **Numerical Example (worked above in Section 3.2):** - 1 m³ of waste cooking oil (920 kg) → 92 kg crude glycerol - MFC at 0.25 W/m², 10 m², 1000 h → **2500 kWh (9000 MJ)** additional energy - Net energy benefit: **~29% additional energy recovery** over biodiesel alone **Process Flow:** 1. Crude glycerol from biodiesel plant → filter (remove residual oil/methanol) → dilute to 5–10 g/L COD 2. Feed to MFC anode chamber with electrogenic biofilm pre-acclimatized to glycerol 3. Continuous operation; HRT = 24–48 h 4. Effluent (treated water) → recycle or discharge 5. Power conditioning (DC-DC converter) → battery storage → load **Benefits of Integration:** - Converts low-value waste (glycerol) to electricity and clean water - Reduces overall carbon footprint of biodiesel plant - Aligns with **circular bioeconomy** principles highlighted in KTU Module 4 - Co-generation of biodiesel (chemical energy) + bioelectricity --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls** > > 1. **Confusing saponification with transesterification**: Saponification is the side reaction where FFA + NaOH → soap + water. Marks lost for not distinguishing. > 2. **Forgetting the 3:1 stoichiometry**: Many students write "triglyceride + 1 methanol → 1 FAME". Always **3:1** stoichiometry, with **6:1–12:1** actual. > 3. **Not stating the catalyst role**: The catalyst does not "speed up" alone; it generates the **methoxide ion (CH₃O⁻)** which is the actual nucleophile. > 4. **Mixing generations of feedstock**: First-generation (edible) and second-generation (non-edible) must be clearly distinguished in tabular form. > 5. **Biodiesel ≠ raw vegetable oil**: Raw oil cannot be used in modern engines due to **high viscosity (~50 mm²/s)** and **coking**. Biodiesel viscosity is 1.9–6.0 mm²/s. > 6. **Energy density comparison**: B100 has ~90% energy density of petro-diesel; stating "more energy" is **factually wrong**. > 7. **Forgetting FFA pre-treatment step**: For 2nd-gen feedstocks, students must mention **acid pre-esterification (H₂SO₄ + methanol)** before base catalysis. > 8. **MFC-biodiesel linkage**: When asked about integration, students must mention **glycerol as a substrate**, not "biomass" in general. Glycerol is the key MFC substrate from biodiesel. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 — Module 4 Rapid Revision Checklist** ### ✅ Core Concepts - **Biodiesel** = mono-alkyl esters of long-chain fatty acids (FAME if methanol, FAEE if ethanol). - **Transesterification** = triglyceride + 3 ROH → 3 FAME + glycerol (catalyst required). - **Stoichiometric ratio** = 3:1 alcohol:oil; **practical ratio** = 6:1 to 12:1. - **Base catalysts**: NaOH, KOH, NaOCH₃ — work on **refined, low-FFA (<2%)** oils. - **Acid catalysts**: H₂SO₄ — used in **pre-esterification of high-FFA** feedstocks. - **Enzymatic (lipase)**: Mild, reusable, expensive; suited for high-FFA feedstocks. - **Supercritical methanol**: 250–400 °C, 10–25 MPa; no catalyst; very high energy input. - **Glycerol** is the by-product (~10 wt% of oil); a key MFC substrate. ### ✅ Feedstock Generations - **1st gen**: Edible oils → food vs fuel conflict. - **2nd gen**: Jatropha, Karanja, WCO → sustainable, may have high FFA. - **3rd gen**: Oleaginous microbes (*Chlorella*, *Yarrowia*, *Rhodotorula*) → 20–70% lipid content. - **4th gen**: Genetically engineered microbes/algae. ### ✅ Key Fuel Properties - **Density**: 0.86–0.90 g/cm³ (ASTM D6751). - **Kinematic viscosity (40 °C)**: 1.9–6.0 mm²/s. - **Cetane number**: ≥ 47 (B100). - **Flash point**: > 130 °C (safer than petro-diesel). - **Sulfur content**: < 10 ppm (near-zero). - **Oxygen content**: 10–12 wt%. ### ✅ Critical Process Parameters 1. Molar ratio alcohol:oil = **6:1 to 12:1** 2. Temperature = **55–65 °C** (just below MeOH boiling point) 3. Catalyst = **0.5–1.5 wt% NaOH** (base) or **1–5 wt% H₂SO₄** (acid) 4. FFA content for base catalysis = **< 2%** (else use two-step) 5. Moisture content = **< 0.5 wt%** (else saponification) 6. Reaction time = **30 min – 1 h** (base) or **4–8 h** (acid) 7. Mixing = **> 600 rpm** to overcome mass-transfer limitations ### ✅ MFC–Biodiesel Integration - **Glycerol** from biodiesel → MFC anode substrate. - **Microbial lipids** from oleaginous microbes → biodiesel feedstock. - Combined **biorefinery** = MFC (electricity) + biodiesel (fuel) + glycerol recycle. - Net energy benefit example: **~29% additional energy recovery**. ### ✅ Examiner's Quick-Fire Facts - India: **SATAT scheme** for biodiesel from UCO. - India's biodiesel blending target: **5% by 2030**. - Global standards: **ASTM D6751** (USA), **EN 14214** (Europe), **IS 15607** (India). - Biofuel Policy India 2018 categorizes biodiesel under **2nd & 3rd gen biofuels**. - **Glycerol purity** from biodiesel plant: crude (80–85%) → refined (>99.7%, USP grade). ### ✅ Common Equations to Memorize - Transesterification stoichiometry (3:1) - FAME yield: $\eta_{\text{FAME}} = \dfrac{m_{\text{FAME}}}{m_{\text{theoretical}}} \times 100$ - Lipid content: $Y_{\text{lipid}} = \dfrac{m_{\text{lipid}}}{m_{\text{biomass,dry}}} \times 100$ - Cetane number estimation: $\text{CN} \approx 46.3 + \dfrac{5458}{\text{SV}} - 0.225 \times \text{IV}$ - Saponification value: $\text{SV} = \dfrac{56.1 \times V \times N}{m}$ - MFC energy: $E_{\text{MFC}} = P \times A \times t$ - Net biorefinery energy: $E_{\text{net}} = E_{\text{biodiesel}} + E_{\text{MFC}} - E_{\text{input}}$
